questions.title: Asset dependencies across code locations

Asset dependencies across code locations

```

def A():

```

@asset

  ...
defs = Definitions(assets=[B])

The docs used to recommend using SourceAssets for this (before that I think it was asset sensors?), but references to SourceAssets seem to have been quietly removed from the docs. Is the new approach to use External Assets? Something else? Any ideas are very welcome!

questions.title: Code location instability - gRPC Error code: UNAVAILABLE
questions.question:
Code location instability - gRPC Error code: UNAVAILABLE

I've recently increased the number of concurrent jobs running on Dagster, and I've noticed that the code location has become less stable than before. I'm encountering errors such as `dagster._core.errors.DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: Could not reach the user code server. gRPC Error code: UNAVAILABLE`. It seems that the gRPC server connection intermittently disconnects, approximately every 10-20 minutes. This connectivity issue is causing existing jobs to abruptly fail with `dagster._core.errors.DagsterExecutionInterruptedError` errors. It seems that I'm getting this error bcos it is failing the `dagster api grpc-health-check`

What can I do to enhance the stability of the code location?

<!--Example of error:
![image](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/assets/52529078/c5acaffe-08a8-4c44-8d7d-c0fcaa8b60bf)
-->
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @ytoast . The `dagster._core.errors.DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: Could not reach the user code server. gRPC Error code: UNAVAILABLE` means that the process trying to connect to the user code server couldn't. This could be due to any number of reasons depending on your deployment setup. As for the job failures - the executing jobs don't communicate with the user code server, so the `UNAVAILABLE` error isn't causing the job failures, but the cause of the `UNAVAILABLE` error could also be causing the jobs to fail. 

You'll likely need to use the debugging tools for your particular deployment type to debug why the processes are failing
questions.curate_reason: Concrete gRPC stability question with substantive answer; chunk [6] provides relevant configuration guidance about worker limits and bottlenecks under load.

questions.title: Is there a Celery-specific RunLauncher (without k8s)?
questions.question:
Is there a Celery-specific RunLauncher (without k8s)?

[I'm not seeing a Celery RunLauncher](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/run-launcher), but I'm seeing a [celery executor](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/libraries/dagster-celery#api), which I guess you can launch with any of the run launchers? I'm confused as to why there's a [CeleryK8sRunLauncher](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/libraries/dagster-celery-k8s#dagster_celery_k8s.CeleryK8sRunLauncher) in the first place, because it seems like that run launcher uses the celery broker as the queue, and then k8s as the worker/executor. It seems more accurate to think of the Celery broker/backend as the RunCoordinator?
questions.reference_answer:
Using the CeleryK8sRunLauncher is required if you're using the celery_k8s_job_executor, which is discussed here and lets you use celery to maintain global concurrency limits across multiple runs: https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/guides/kubernetes/deploying-with-helm-advanced#per-op-limits-in-kubernetes

I think if we were redoing the celery+k8s integration from scratch in 2023 we probably would not have a separate run launcher for this, and would instead just use the K8sRunLauncher there as well, as in practice they do the same thing (launch the run worker for a given run in a k8s pod)
questions.curate_reason: Concrete question about component existence; chunks confirm CeleryK8sRunLauncher is the only Celery-specific run launcher and explain its purpose for maintaining concurrency limits across runs.

questions.title: How can I use the cli correctly？
questions.question:
How can I use the cli correctly？

Hi, 

As the title asks, I created a project based on the documentation and ran it, and then I found the cli command in the dagster documentation API class. I tried to debug my project using the cli, but I found that using the cli (e.g. dagster job execute -m project1 --job test1_job) generated a completely new instance, I can't find these requests in the dagster websever which I have run. 

What should I do?I want to run multiple jobs through cli commands and have them reflect on the dagster core or dagster ui that I have already run. I want to use this to investigate how dagster schedules multiple job requests.

Look forward to hearing from you. Any reply will be greatly appreciated.
questions.reference_answer:
To ensure that job executions from the CLI are reflected in the Dagster UI, you need to make sure that both the CLI and the Dagster UI (webserver) are using the same Dagster instance. This is typically managed by setting the `DAGSTER_HOME` environment variable to point to the same directory for both processes.

1. **Set the `DAGSTER_HOME` Environment Variable**: Ensure that the `DAGSTER_HOME` environment variable is set to the same location in the environment where you're running the Dagster webserver and the CLI commands. This environment variable tells Dagster where to store instance-related data, including run history, event logs, and configuration.

2. **Check the Instance Configuration**: Verify that the `dagster.yaml` file in the `DAGSTER_HOME` directory is correctly configured and that both the CLI and webserver are indeed using the same instance configuration.

3. **Use the Same Python Environment**: Make sure that the Python environment from which you're running the CLI commands is the same environment where the Dagster webserver is running. This ensures that they have access to the same Dagster packages and configuration.

4. **Check Network Configuration**: If you're running the webserver and the CLI on different machines or containers, ensure that they can communicate over the network and that there are no firewall or network policy issues preventing them from accessing the same Dagster instance.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [2] directly explains how DAGSTER_HOME determines which instance Dagster uses and why temporary storage is created when unset; chunk [4] supports the DAGSTER_HOME solution.

questions.title: Multiple code locations in Dagster OSS with shared code (resources, utils)
questions.question:
Multiple code locations in Dagster OSS with shared code (resources, utils)

Hi all,
I’m looking for some guidance on repo structure best practices in Dagster OSS.

**Context:**
- I have **multiple code locations**, each of which requires its **own Python environment**
- I have a set of **shared utilities, resources** that are used across all code locations.

Current repo structure (simplified):

dagster_OSS/
├─ analytics/        # code location 1 (unique env)
│   ├─ defs/
│   ├─ lib/
|   └─ pyproject.toml
├─ data_engineering/          # code location 2 (unique env)
│   ├─ defs/
│   ├─ lib/
│   ├─ resources/             # shared resources
│   ├─ utils/                 # shared utilities
|   └─ pyproject.toml
├─ deploy_files/
├─ workspace.yaml             # defines the code locations
└─ pyproject.toml

Each code location may need libraries that would clash with others, so isolation is important. But I also want to avoid duplicating the shared utils/ and resources/ code across locations.

**What’s the recommended way to structure repos when you need per-location env isolation but also shared code?**
questions.reference_answer:
I would highly recommend using [uv workspaces](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/concepts/projects/workspaces/) for this, where each code location is a workspace and shared packages can be represented one or more workspaces within the same project. This makes it trivial to share common code across code locations. Just make sure all you don't have any shared code workspaces importing from code locations so as to avoid circular dependencies.

My company has a monorepo with about a half dozen code locations and a half dozen internal packages all structured as workspaces within a single UV project and it works amazingly well... within the codebase it feels like it's all just one big package but we can easily build and deploy code locations individually and can also slice out reusable shared code packages that are useful outside of Dagster as well.

Edit: just wanted to note that I personally try to avoid importing code from one code location into another code location, as those kinds of dependencies will cause scenarios where updating one code location requires also redeploying another code location. This can easily get you into weird situations where code location B is running with out-of-date code imported from an old version of code location A, because someone updated location A but didn't know they had to restart B as well (among other suboptimal scenarios).
questions.curate_reason: Concrete question about Dagster monorepo structure with multiple code locations and shared code; chunks provide relevant guidance on workspaces, sharing code across locations, and configuring separate Python environments.

questions.title: Structured / json logging by default in Dagster+
questions.question:
Structured / json logging by default in Dagster+

What is the recommended route to enable Dagster to emit structured logs in Dagster+ by default? If you add a logger to the `loggers` argument of your `Definitions` object, the logger becomes available to your jobs, but must be opted-into by adding some configuration to the launchpad. This can't really be pre-configured with the `config=` argument of jobs if your configuration is dynamically set at runtime, which is the case for most of my jobs. It seems like the user needs to [configure the settings](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/logging/python-logging#configuring-python-log-handlers-) in `dagster.yaml` - but for Dagster+, this is configured as part of the Dagster Agent container (at least for ECS hybrid deployment). So it appears that to get a custom logger to be used by default for Dagster jobs and event logs one needs to make a custom Dagster Agent container with their custom logger included and change the `dagster.yaml` config to point to the custom log handler with my formatter already attached to it. Is this correct? Or is there a `class` argument to the `formatter` block that I could use to point to a custom formatter? Do I even need to add the custom logging code to the Dagster Agent container since it's mostly just launching the user code container? Or is there another way entirely to extend logging in Dagster+? 

I've had a few discussions with Dagster team members who have mentioned there are some vague plans for improving this, but no one has been able to give me guidance on how exactly to set up custom loggers with the current Dagster implementation. Even if it's suboptimal, I'd really appreciate some guidance on how this is meant to be configured right now. There are [multiple issues](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/20084) and [discussions](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/14258) up right now with no actionable input from Dagster team members on how to solve this problem, non-standard logging feels very neglected right now.

Of note, I'm using the ECS hybrid deployment right now. Willing to switch to k8s if it can get me custom logging, this is really killing a lot of plans I have to introduce much better visibility into our Dagster deployments and better tie Dagster logs to external compute logs and metrics. It sounds like any sort of first-class structured logging support is still a long ways out with no concrete plans in place right now, so even if someone can just provide a workaround while improvements are made that would be incredibly helpful. Unless of course it's just plain impossible right now, which would also be good to know so people don't spend tons of time on it.
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @zyd14 - firstly apologies for the bad experiences here. I'd like to see if we can fix the confusing `loggers` override behavior that you and many others have run into and sent out a PR to attempt to start that process here: https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/24338. I suspect the main blocker there is how to enable that behavior (which seems much more intuitive to me) without it being a breaking change for existing loggers.

To get a custom logger to be used by default in your Dagster+ jobs, you don't need to build a custom agent container. It's a little confusing because you specify it in the agent's dagster.yaml, but the configuration there is only read in your user code container so it should only need to be defined there.

And you can use a custom formatter class - I sent out https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/24331/ to see if we can make the Dagster-compatible JSON logging formatter that the `json_console_logger` exportable as a top-level class. Once that is live, you could put something this in your agent dagster.yaml in  ECS:

```
python_logs:
  dagster_handler_config:
    handlers:
      console:
        class: logging.StreamHandler
        formatter: json
    formatters:
      json:
       '()': dagster.JsonFormatter
```

(or a different custom formatter class of your choosing). When I tried that, it added JSON formatted logs like this to the job output (in addition to the default system console logger for the jobs).

One additional benefit of that approach (setting it in the dagster.yaml rather than in python code) is that it will still use that formatter for the initial events that happen before we have had a chance to load the job code yet.
questions.curate_reason: The question is a concrete, self-contained configuration question about enabling custom loggers in Dagster+, and the accepted answer provides substantive guidance on using dagster.yaml to configure handlers and formatters, which is supported by documentation chunks [2] and [3] that explain Python logging configuration via dagster.yaml.

questions.title: Multiprocess slow start for ops
questions.question:
Multiprocess slow start for ops

Hi All

With dagster in production we're seeing ops take 5+ seconds to start, even if nothing else is running at the same time.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/225131/162109152-15f8bf7f-d672-475c-a20e-3234872e97a2.png)

We're using:
Python 3.8.12 - just upgraded to 3.10.4 and it has the same issue
Dagster: 0.14.7
Backing store: Postgres (AWS RDS)
Host: Ubuntu on AWS EC2 Graviton2 (but also has issues on AMD64) `Linux ip-xxx 5.13.0-1017-aws #19~20.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Mon Mar 7 12:55:31 UTC 2022 aarch64 aarch64 aarch64 GNU/Linux)` Ubuntu 20.04.4 LTS
questions.reference_answer:
One tool to avoid repeating the cost is to use the `start_method` `forkserver` which is available as config on the multiprocess executor. The first op will still pay the init cost to create the template process, but each subsequent op should be faster as it forks that template process instead of starting from scratch. You may need to explicitly set `preload_modules` in the config if the default behavior doesn't load the necessary modules. 

context: https://docs.python.org/3/library/multiprocessing.html#contexts-and-start-methods
questions.curate_reason: Concrete multiprocess executor configuration question with substantive answer; chunks [0] and [1] directly discuss using forkserver to reduce per-process overhead during multiprocess execution.

questions.title: Confusion about the Dagster hybrid deployment: agents, launchers, executors, workers
questions.question:
Confusion about the Dagster hybrid deployment: agents, launchers, executors, workers

I'm finding it difficult to follow all of the deployment-related documentation (though it's definitely helpful), because there's overlapping docs and concepts spread across [OSS](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/overview#job-execution-flow), serverless, [hybrid](https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-cloud/deployment/agents), and then all 3. Here's my current understanding. 

[Daemon](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/dagster-daemon) - long-running process needed for schedules, sensors, queued run coordinator, monitoring worker runs and triggering retries

[Storage](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/dagster-instance#dagster-storage)
History of [runs](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/dagster-instance#dagster-storage) - Sqlite, postgres, mysql, can be set to local or remote db
[Logs](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/dagster-instance#compute-log-storage) from op compute functions - s3, gcs
[Output from ops](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/dagster-instance#local-artifact-storage) - where to store outputs that are written to a filesystem 

[gRPC server](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/dagster-instance#grpc-servers) - loads code that defines our Dagster assets (ops, jobs, assets, schedules, etc) and makes metadata about those assets available via a GraphQL API 

Execution
[Run coordinator](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/run-coordinator) -> run launcher -> run worker -> executor 
Run coordinator - a class invoked by either the webserver or a Dagster api request. This class can be configured to pass runs to the daemon via a queue. The coordinator determines the policy used to set the prioritization rules and concurrency limits for runs. 

[Run launcher](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/run-launcher) - a class invoked by the daemon when it receives a run from the coordinator. This class initializes a new run worker to handle execution. Depending on the launcher, this could mean spinning up a new process, container, Kubernetes pod, etc.
Run worker -  a process which traverses a graph and uses the executor to execute each op.
[Executor](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/executors) - a class invoked by the run worker for running user ops. Depending on the executor, ops run in local processes, new containers, Kubernetes pods, etc.

[Web server](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/webserver/ui) - UI for visualizing jobs, assets, and ops, as well as starting jobs.

Some questions.

Dagster hosts the following in Dagster Cloud:

- Daemon 
- Run storage
- Log storage? 
- GraphQL API
- Web server
- Run coordinator? 
- Anything else?

The user hosts:

- Agent
- Run launcher
- Run worker
- Executor

I get that the Agent relays data to/from Dagster Cloud. Does it also serve the role of the run launcher? 

What happens with the DefaultRunLauncher if no executors are available? Does the op get queued using the runs database? 

How do you configure the Run Worker? I'm not seeing a section on that. I'm a little confused by how a run launcher triggers one or more run workers, which in turn trigger one or more executors? 

In general, I'm confused on how the [job execution flow](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/overview#job-execution-flow) works within a hybrid deployment. Is it possible to use a [celery executor](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/guides/celery#execute-on-celery) with a hybrid deployment? Is the Dagster-hosted run storage accessible outside of Dagster cloud? That seems like the only way that it would work. 

Thanks!
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @skeller88 - the overall understanding you lay out here is more or less entirely correct. Happy to mop up the specific questions here:

- The agent does serve the role of run launcher. It spins up an isolated task/pod/process/etc. for each run based on instructions that it pulls from our API. 
- The default executor if you don't pick one is to run each op in its own subprocess. More here: https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/ops-jobs-graphs/job-execution#default-job-executor
- The specific knobs available to configure the run worker vary considerably depending on which agent you're using (or what environment you're deploying to in OSS). We have a k8s agent configuration reference here, for example: https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-cloud/deployment/agents/kubernetes/configuration-reference#kubernetes-agent-configuration-reference and and ECS agent reference here: https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-cloud/deployment/agents/amazon-ecs/configuration-reference#amazon-ecs-agent-configuration-reference
- This doc might be helpful to explain the job execution flow in hybrid: https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-cloud/deployment/hybrid#hybrid-architecture-overview - once a run actually starts, its executing in the agent's environment in its own task/pod/etc. and writing metadata back to our servers over our API.
- We don't currently support the celery executor in dagster cloud. Since we first shipped our celery integration we've significantly improved our built-in concurrency support which covers a lot of the same bases that would lead somebody to use celery: https://docs.dagster.io/guides/limiting-concurrency-in-data-pipelines#limiting-concurrency-in-data-pipelines - so we haven't seen much demand for celery support in Dagster Cloud.

Let us know if there's anything else we can help with!
questions.curate_reason: Multiple chunks directly explain the job execution flow, run coordinators, hybrid architecture, and run workers/executors that form the core deployment concepts verified in the maintainer's answer.

questions.title: Unit Testing ConfigurableResource Classes
questions.reference_answer:
Playing around with this. If you patch the method on the class before instantiating the configurable resource, that avoids the Pydantic reassignment issue.

Slightly cleaning up the original code so it will run:
```python
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import Field
import dagster as dg
from pydantic import BaseModel
import requests
from typing import List, Dict


class ApiToken(BaseModel):
    value: str = Field(description="The API token value")
    has_expired: bool = Field(description="Whether the token has expired")


class RestApiClient(dg.ConfigurableResource):
    _api_token: Optional[ApiToken] = None

    @property
    def api_token(self) -> ApiToken:
        if not self._api_token or self._api_token.has_expired():
            self._api_token = self.refresh_access_token()
        return self._api_token

    def refresh_access_token(self) -> ApiToken:
        # Code for handling a POST call for an API token value
        return "my_key"

    def get_paginated(self, api_path: str) -> List[Dict]:
        headers = {}
        request_url = f"https://api.example.com/{api_path}"
        headers["Authorization"] = "Bearer {}".format(self.api_token.value)

        response = requests.Session().get(request_url, headers=headers)
        response.raise_for_status()
```

You will still need to mock a number of things but you can still follow everything that is happening:

```python
from example.defs.resources import RestApiClient, ApiToken

from unittest.mock import patch, Mock


@patch("example.defs.resources.RestApiClient.refresh_access_token")
def test_calls_refresh_access_token_if_no_access_token(mock_refresh_access_token):
    # Mock Api Token
    mock_token = Mock(ApiToken)
    mock_token.value = "test_key"
    mock_token.has_expired = False

    mock_refresh_access_token.return_value = mock_token

    # Construct after patch
    client = RestApiClient()
    mock_response = Mock()

    with patch("requests.Session.get") as mock_get:
        mock_get.return_value = mock_response
        client.get_paginated("test")

    mock_refresh_access_token.assert_called_once()
    mock_get.assert_called_once_with(
        "https://api.example.com/test", headers={"Authorization": "Bearer test_key"}
    )
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete dagster question about unit testing ConfigurableResource classes with a substantive solution; chunks 6-7 are from the testing-configurable-resources.md documentation which should cover this testing pattern.

How to programmatically determine logKey for capturedLogsMetadata GraphQL query?

I'm building internal tooling to programmatically retrieve compute logs for failed Dagster runs via the GraphQL API. I can see how the UI fetches logs using the `capturedLogsMetadata` query, but I'm struggling to understand how to construct the required `logKey` argument.

### What I have observed

questions.title: How to add private Python packages to a Dagster cloud deployment?
questions.question:
How to add private Python packages to a Dagster cloud deployment?

If I have a private GitHub repository, eg. `my-org/private-repo` and I want to include a Python package from that repository in our dagster cloud project, how can I do that?
questions.reference_answer:
If you use fast deploys in your workflow, the following steps can install a private package as a dependency to your dagster cloud project. If you have `ENABLE_FAST_DEPLOYS: "true"` in your `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` then you use fast deploys.

1.  In your `deploy.yml` file, add the following to the top of `steps:` section in the `dagster-cloud-default-deploy` job.
    ```yaml
      - name: Checkout internal repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v3
        with:
          token: ${{ secrets.GH_PAT }}
          repository: my-org/private-repo
          path: deps/private-repo
          ref: some-branch   # optional to check out a specific branch
      
      - name: Build a wheel
        # adjust the `cd` command to cd into the directory with setup.py
        run: >
          cd deps/private-repo &&
          python setup.py bdist_wheel &&
          mkdir -p $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/deps &&
          cp dist/*whl $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/deps
                    

      # If you have multiple private packages, the above two steps should be repeated for each but the following step is only
      # needed once
      - name: Configure dependency resolution to use the wheel built above
        run: >
          echo "[global]" > $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/deps/pip.conf &&
          echo "find-links = " >> $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/deps/pip.conf &&
          echo "    file://$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/deps/" >> $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/deps/pip.conf &&
          echo "PIP_CONFIG_FILE=$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/deps/pip.conf" > $GITHUB_ENV
    ```
2. Create a github personal access token and set it as the `GH_PAT` secret for your actions.
3. In your dagster project's `setup.py` add your package name to the `install_requires` section:
    ```python
        install_requires=[
          "dagster",
          "dagster-cloud",
          "private-package",   # add this line - must match your private Python package name
    ```

Once the `deploy.yml` is updated, any code deploy should checkout your private repository, build the package and install it as a dependency in your dagster cloud project. Repeat the above steps for `branch_deployments.yml`.
questions.curate_reason: Chunks 0, 3, 8, and 9 from the 'Use private Python packages' documentation section contain the workflow steps (checkout, build wheel, configure pip) and explanation needed to add private GitHub packages to Dagster Cloud deployments.

questions.title: How do I run a downstream op even if an upstream op fails?
questions.question:
How do I run a downstream op even if an upstream op fails?

Sources from slack: https://dagster.slack.com/archives/C01U954MEER/p1693262962775569

What is the best way to model non-strict dependencies? That is, I have an operation op that takes the result of some other (potentially complex) subgraphs (let’s say three subgraphs a, b and c) and combines them into one result. So, op only fails if all of the subgraphs a, b and c fail. But if, for example, a fails but b and c both return a result, op still succeeds and returns a result that combines the results of b and c.

Now, since each one of a , b and c in this example can be complex graphs which can fail at different stages of the computation, I need to model op so that it waits until, for each of a, b and c , either the result is ready or it is determined that they cannot have a result and then op is run and the available results for a , b and c are passed to it.

The potential use case for this is. e.g., when you want to do ensemble learning and combine predictors of different estimations for the same problem. So, if one predictor fails and cannot provide a value, it should not stop the whole prediction process.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk 0 on fixed fan-in directly supports the core pattern in the answer for combining multiple op outputs with non-required dependencies.

questions.title: dagster-dbt is not respecting`--indirect-selection cautious` test selection
questions.question:
dagster-dbt is not respecting`--indirect-selection cautious` test selection

Let's say I have two dbt models: `model_a` and `model_b`. Both models are referenced by a test called `test_model_a_and_b`.

Based on dbt documentation, [indirect selection](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/test-selection-examples?indirect-selection-mode=cautious#indirect-selection):
```
These are the modes to configure the behavior when performing indirect selection (with eager as the default):

- eager (default) - include ANY test that references the selected nodes
- cautious - restrict to tests that ONLY refer to selected nodes
- buildable - restrict to tests that ONLY refer to selected nodes (or their ancestors)
- empty - restrict to tests that are only for the selected node and ignore all tests from the attached nodes
```

When doing dbt build with dbt-core: `dbt build --select model_a --indirect-selection cautious`, test `test_model_a_and_b` is not run. It matches the behaviour described above, cautious, restricts to tests that ONLY refer to selected nodes.

When doing dbt build with dbt-core: `dbt build --select model_a model_b --indirect-selection cautious`, test `test_model_a_and_b` is run.

However on Dagster, when `model_a` were to auto-materialise on its own, `test_model_a_and_b` is run. Same is true when `model_b` were to auto-materialise on its own.

Is this behaviour expected? Seems like Dagster is not respecting dbt indirect test selection.

Below is how I am loading dbt models as Dagster assets:
```
def base_dbt_models() -> List:
    translator = CustomDagsterDbtTranslator(
        settings=DagsterDbtTranslatorSettings(enable_asset_checks=True)
    )
    dbt_assets_list = []
    # Only load assets from dbt project in Dagster SG instance.
    if is_region_SG:
        # hot reload dbt assets for local development
        if is_dev:
            dbt_parse_invocation = dbt_resource.cli(["parse", "--write-json"]).wait()
            dbt_manifest_path = dbt_parse_invocation.target_path.joinpath("manifest.json")
        else:
            dbt_manifest_path = file_relative_path(__file__, "target/manifest.json")

        @dbt_assets(manifest=dbt_manifest_path, dagster_dbt_translator=translator)
        def base_dbt_models_dbt_assets(
            context: AssetExecutionContext,
            dbt: DbtCliResource,
            config: DbtConfig
        ):
            dbt_build_args = ["build", "--indirect-selection", "cautious"]
            if config.full_refresh:
                dbt_build_args += ["--full-refresh"]
            yield from dbt.cli(dbt_build_args, context=context).stream()

        dbt_assets_list.append(base_dbt_models_dbt_assets)
    return dbt_assets_list
```
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @bolinzzz, when Dagster asset checks are enabled, we override the dbt indirect selection setting to empty, then pass the exact set of tests we want to run.

It's possible with sensors/schedules to customize which tests run. However currently with Auto materialize, every check on the asset will run with the materialization. We have a tracking issue to customize asset checks + auto materializing here: https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/16719. 

Another relevant issue is to be able to exclude certain tests from being ingested with @dbt_assets: https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/18124

Currently to stick with auto materializing and to not execute this test, you'll have to disable dbt asset checks until we close one of these issues.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [0] directly explains that Dagster overrides DBT_INDIRECT_SELECTION to 'empty' for explicit test selection, which is the technical reason given in the answer; chunk [1] covers the workaround of disabling asset checks.

questions.title: How to load dbt source description and metadata into Dagster
questions.question:
How to load dbt source description and metadata into Dagster

I have successfully integrated a dbt model into Dagster. Description and metadata are correctly pulled in by Dagster for my tables and visible both in the graph (the description) and the corresponding asset detail pages . However, `source` details do not seem to be populated when I click on the asset details. The model runs nicely via Dagster and sources appear in the graph. 

My dummy dbt `schema.yml` is as follows:
```
version: 2

models:
  - name: test_model_1
    description: "This model contains user-level revenue data aggregated at the monthly level."
    columns:
      - name: n_of_rows
    config:
      meta:
        dagster:
          group: revenue

sources:
  - name: external
    description: "This is an external source"
    database: ext
    schema: ext
    tables:
    - name: payments
      description: "User payment data"
      meta:
        dagster:
          group: revenue
```

More specifically:
- I do get to see "This model contains user-level revenue data aggregated at the monthly level." for my `test_model_1` in the graph node and  on the asset profile.
- But I do not see "User payment data" either in the graph node or the asset profile.
questions.reference_answer:
Details like description and column metadata are not automatically pulled in for dbt sources. By default, Dagster only takes the dbt source's asset key and displays it in the asset graph.

If you'd like these details to be visible for a dbt source, then you should:

1. Define your dbt source as a Dagster asset (using `@asset` or `@multi_asset`)
2. Define the metadata for that Dagster asset using the dbt manifest.

### References
- https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/dbt/reference#upstream-dependencies
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [1]'s section title directly addresses the core recommended solution of defining dbt sources as Dagster assets.

questions.title: How to unit-test `asset_sensor`s
questions.question:
How to unit-test `asset_sensor`s

Imagine you have an asset sensor defined as:

```
@asset_sensor(asset_key=AssetKey("foo"))
def my_sensor(context):
    ...
```

You may want to unit-test this sensor to make sure it behaves as expected. You can evaluate a tick of a sensor in the context of a unit test using `build_sensor_context`. However, asset sensors rely on querying for new events from the DagsterInstance, so you'll need to provide an `instance` parameter when building your context.

So the question becomes, how can you get a DagsterInstance that's suitable for unit tests?
questions.reference_answer:
The solution starts off by using an ephemeral DagsterInstance (i.e. it will only exist for the duration of the test), and passing that into the `build_sensor_context` method:

```
from dagster import DagsterInstance, build_sensor_context

def test_my_sensor():
    with DagsterInstance.ephemeral() as instance:
        context = build_sensor_context(..., instance=instance)
        # note: we use list() here because my_sensor is actually a generator, so we need to exhaust the generator
        # to make sure our code runs
        result = list(my_sensor(context))
```

However, if you run this, your sensor code will not execute at all, as the sensor only runs the custom code when there are new AssetMaterialization events for the requested key. Because we just created this DagsterInstance, it will have no events in it.

To get past this, we'll need to materialize an asset. Depending on how complex your sensor code is, you can probably get away with just making sure that a materialization event exists for the key you care about, without worrying about how that event is created. This could look like:

```
from dagster import DagsterInstance, build_sensor_context, materialize_to_memory, asset

def test_my_sensor():
    @asset
    def foo():
        pass

    with DagsterInstance.ephemeral() as instance:
        # here, we materialize an asset with key `foo`, so that event will exist on our instance
        materialize_to_memory([foo], instance=instance)
        
        context = build_sensor_context(..., instance=instance)
        result = list(my_sensor(context))
```

For complex use cases, you may want to import the actual definition of the asset you care about and pass that into `materialize_to_memory`, rather than mocking it in the test.
questions.curate_reason: The question is a concrete, valid Dagster API question; chunk [4] from testing-sensors.md explicitly discusses unit testing sensors with build_sensor_context and direct invocation, which are core to the accepted answer.

questions.title: How to get 1-to-1 DeclarativeAutomation.eager() with partitioned asset?
questions.question: How to get 1-to-1 DeclarativeAutomation.eager() with partitioned asset? Consider an asset, `A`, with that feeds into a `multi_asset` that produces `B` and `C` which are both _daily_ partitioned. `A` is triggered on a schedule and `B` and `C` both have `DeclarativeAutomation.eager()`. However, the automation sensor never triggers a materialization for `B` or `C`. Can someone provide an example of how to get this to work? If I look at the automation logic check for either `B` or `C`, it looks like the conditions `NOT any_deps_missing`, `NOT any_deps_in_progress`, and `NOT in_progress` are not _evaluated_ on any partitions ("partitions evaluated = 0") and by default return False. This causes the automation condition to fail.
questions.reference_answer:
OK; for posterity, here is how you have a 1-to-1 mapping of eager declarative automation between partitioned assets (so that an update to partition `2024-04-02` triggers a child's `2024-04-02` partition to be materialize).

Use `automation_condition=AutomationCondition.eager().without(AutomationCondition.in_latest_time_window())`.
questions.curate_reason: The accepted answer is a concrete API configuration solution for partitioned asset automation; chunk [4] titled 'Updating older time partitions' directly addresses how to remove the time window restriction that prevents older partitions from triggering.

questions.title: Handle assets that are too big to fit in memory
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @AlejandroUPC 

How you solve this will depend on where you are persisting data, but nothing is stopping you from batching within an asset itself. I would suggest a slight change to your assets, as they appear to be written as tasks right now (e.g. read from stream, process message, write to db). An asset should reflect the thing you care about, which in this case sounds like a processed message.

Using your example, you might consider having one asset to manage reading, processing, and writing. 

```
from dagster import asset

@asset(deps=[some_source])
def processed_messages(stream: StreamResource, storage: StorageResource):
    for batch in stream.get_batch(): 
        processed = process_batch(batch)
        storage.save_files(processed)
```

You will want to create resources, here's an example of a resource for the StreamResource, but the StorageResource would be similar.
```
class MyStreamResource(ConfigurableResource):
    username: str
    password: str

    def get_batch(n=1000, ...):
        client = SomeClient(self.username, self.password)
        cursor = None
        while True:
             batch = client.get_some_records(after: cursor) 
             cursor = batch['cursor']
             yield batch['data']
```

and so on.
questions.curate_reason: Question asks about handling large assets with batching; answer recommends combining ETL phases into a single asset with resources, which is directly addressed by the modeling-etl-pipelines guide, resource-usage documentation, and guidance on combining assets.

questions.title: My Dagster code is hanging or taking longer than I expect to execute. How can I troubleshoot?
questions.question:
My Dagster code is hanging or taking longer than I expect to execute. How can I troubleshoot?

- An op is taking longer than I would expect to run, how can I find the bottleneck?
- A dagster run is hanging and I want to know where it's stuck
- A code server has become unresponsive but doesn't seem to be memory or CPU-bound, why is it stuck?
questions.reference_answer:
We recommend that you use [py-spy](https://github.com/benfred/py-spy) to profile your code. 

For hanging code, `py-spy dump` can give you a dump of each thread, which usually makes it immediately clear where the hang is happening.

For slow code, `py-spy record` can produce file that gives you a flame graph of where the process is spending the most time. (We recommend `py-spy record -f speedscope --idle` to produce [speedscope](https://github.com/jlfwong/speedscope) profiles, and to include idle CPU time in the results)

Note that py-spy usually requires elevated permissions in order to run.

For example, to run py-spy locally and understand why definitions are taking a long time to import:

```
sudo py-spy record -f speedscope --idle -- dagster definitions validate
```

--

A typical workflow for generating a py-spy dump for a hanging run in Kubernetes:

a) Set up your Dagster deployment so that each run pod is using a security context that can run py-spy https://github.com/benfred/py-spy#how-do-i-run-py-spy-in-kubernetes

For example, you can set this in your dagster_cloud.yaml file for your code location if you're running a Kubernetes agent to make both your code servers and run pods able to work with py-spy:

```
locations:
  - location_name: cloud-examples
    image: dagster/dagster-cloud-examples:latest
    code_source:
      package_name: dagster_cloud_examples
    container_context:
      k8s:
        server_k8s_config: # Raw kubernetes config for code servers launched by the agent
          container_config:
            securityContext:
              capabilities:
                add:
                  - SYS_PTRACE
        run_k8s_config: # Raw kubernetes config for runs launched by the agent
          container_config:
            securityContext:
              capabilities:
                add:
                  - SYS_PTRACE
```

Or if you're using the Dagster open-source helm chart, you can configure the run launcher to launch each run with 

```
runLauncher:
  type: K8sRunLauncher
  config:
    k8sRunLauncher:
      runK8sConfig:
        containerConfig:
          securityContext:
            capabilities:
              add:
                - SYS_PTRACE
```

(Note that this gives the pod elevated permissions - check with your cluster admins to make sure this is an acceptable change to make temporarily)


See https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-cloud/deployment/agents/kubernetes/configuration-reference#per-location-configuration (Cloud) and https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/guides/kubernetes/customizing-your-deployment#per-job-or-per-op-kubernetes-configuration (OSS) for more information on how to apply these types of customizations to your Kubernetes pods.

b) Launch a run and wait until it hangs

c) Check the event logs for the run to find the run pod, then `kubectl exec` into the pod to run `py-spy`:

```
kubectl exec -it <pod name here> /bin/bash
```

d) install py-spy, then run it:

```
pip install py-spy
py-spy dump --pid 1
```

This should output a dump of what each thread in the process is doing.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete troubleshooting question with substantive answer; chunks 0, 1, 2 directly contain the py-spy recommendation with examples for both local and Kubernetes profiling.

questions.title: Trying to deploy my code location to Dagster+ and encountering "TypeError: 'type' object is not subscriptable`"
questions.question:
Trying to deploy my code location to Dagster+ and encountering "TypeError: 'type' object is not subscriptable`"

When I try to deploy my code location to Dagster+ I'm encountering this below error being thrown by the `fsspec` dependency: 

```
File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fsspec/caching.py", line 40, in <module>
MultiFetcher = Callable[list[[int, int]], bytes] # Maps [(start, end)] to bytes
TypeError: 'type' object is not subscriptable
```
questions.reference_answer:
Looks like you're trying to use Python 3.8, but the `fsspec` dependency is incompatible with Python versions prior to 3.9. Other dependencies may also be incompatible. 

Upgrading would be necessary as [Python 3.8 reached its end-of-life late last year](https://devguide.python.org/versions/) and Dagster is [only compatible with Python 3.9 or higher](https://docs.dagster.io/getting-started/installation).
questions.curate_reason: Concrete error question with substantive answer about Python version incompatibility, supported by chunks confirming Dagster requires Python 3.9+ and 3.10+ for installation.

How can I install drivers like ODBC when deploying my code to Dagster+ Serverless?

When trying to deploy my code to Dagster+ Serverless I encounter this error during my CI/CD process: `ImportError: libodbc.so.2: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory`

I think this is because I am using `pyodbc`(used for Microsoft SQL Server) as a dependency in my code and it requires special drivers be available in the environment it's trying to be installed in.
questions.reference_answer:
You can install that required driver either as part of your CI/CD process by using a [lifecycle hook pre-install script](https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-plus/deployment/deployment-types/serverless/runtime-environment#disable-pex-deploys), or by creating and using [your own custom base image](https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-plus/deployment/deployment-types/serverless/runtime-environment#use-a-different-base-image).

To use a lifecycle hook pre-install script requires [disabling PEX deploys](https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-plus/deployment/deployment-types/serverless/runtime-environment#disable-pex-deploys). If using the GitHub Actions workflow, comment out this line to disable PEX-based deploys:

```yaml
env:
  DAGSTER­_CLOUD­_URL: ...
  DAGSTER­_CLOUD­_API­_TOKEN: ...
  # ENABLE­_FAST­_DEPLOYS: 'true'
```

Then in the root of your repo you can add a shell script named `dagster_cloud_pre_install.sh` that contains the instructions to install the required driver. Example: 

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo "Starting dagster_cloud_pre_install.sh..."

# Update package lists
apt-get update

# Install the unixODBC development package required by pyodbc
apt-get install -y unixodbc-dev

echo "Pre-install script completed successfully."
```

Note that this script requires execute permission. To ensure that, run these commands before committing and pushing the file to your repo: 

```bash
git add dagster_cloud_pre_install.sh
git update-index --chmod=+x dagster_cloud_pre_install.sh
```
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [4] directly contains the code example for disabling PEX deploys, which is the prerequisite step for using the lifecycle hook pre-install script solution described in the answer.

questions.title: How to give execution priority to assets or ops within a run
questions.question:
How to give execution priority to assets or ops within a run

Within a Dagster run, I want Dagster to execute some ops or assets before other ops or assets.
questions.reference_answer:
You can set the "dagster/priority" op tag.  The higher the number, the higher the priority. 0 is the default. Both positive and negative numbers can be used.

A higher priority will never allow a downstream asset or op to be executed before an asset or op that's upstream of it.

Asset example:

```python
@asset(op_tags={"dagster/priority": 1})
def asset1():
    ...
```
Op example:

```python
@op(tags={"dagster/priority": 1})
def op1():
    ...
```
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [1] directly documents the dagster/priority tag for setting priority on op and asset definitions, matching the accepted answer.

questions.title: Is it possible to "stream" data using Dagster ops?
questions.question:
Is it possible to "stream" data using Dagster ops?

Hi everyone, is it possible to "stream" data using Dagster ops? e.g.
```python
def op1():
    while True:
        query external API 
        yield result

def op2(continuous_stream_from_op1):
    for x in continuous_stream_from_op1:
        print(x)
```
where op1 continuously creates output, and op2 continuously receive input from op1? Or do we need to wait until op1 is completed, and then op2 receive all of the inputs in one go?

---

*The question was originally asked in [Dagster Slack](https://dagster.slack.com/archives/C01U954MEER/p1698744156655039?thread_ts=1698744156.655039&cid=C01U954MEER).*
questions.reference_answer:
We currently don't support op to op streaming. Here's a tracking issue: https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/4263

However, you can stream data back to dagster from external API within a single op. You can do so using [Dagster Pipes](https://docs.dagster.io/guides/dagster-pipes). Here's a tutorial of invoking subprocess from dagster and streaming back info from that external process: https://docs.dagster.io/guides/dagster-pipes/subprocess
questions.curate_reason: Chunks document Dagster Pipes, the solution recommended in the answer for streaming data back to Dagster from external processes.

questions.title: dbt project with partitioned and unpartitioned assets
questions.question:
dbt project with partitioned and unpartitioned assets

Hi I have a job with partitioned (time-series data) and unpartitioned assets (i.e. lookup tables), and I have a dbt job that builds models from these assets. If I specify a `partitions_def` to the `dbt_assets` decorator, then my unpartitioned dbt assets end up being partitioned. Is there a workaround to ensure that my partitioned dagster assets map to partitioned dbt assets, and unpartitioned dagster assets to unpartitioned dbt assets?
questions.reference_answer: I worked around this by having two `@dbt_asset` definitions, one with a `partitions_def` and one without. I then used dbt tags to mark which dbt models are partitioned daily (`partitioned_daily`). Then I could assign those models to the partitioned `@dbt_asset` using the `select="tag:partitioned_daily"` parameter. The unpartitioned `@dbt_asset` excludes these models with `exclude="tag:partitioned_daily"`

questions.title: Materialize Assets in specific order (besides deps)
questions.question:
Materialize Assets in specific order (besides deps)

I'm just getting started with Dagster and enjoying it so far but have a question about how to best set things up so that certain assets are materialized in a specific order. I'm doing some basic database table replication from our main database and would like to try to do the replication of a few related tables in a specific order  so as to help preserve referential integrity as much as possible. Think tables along the lines of Event, Team & Member where Events have Teams and Teams have Members and you're constantly getting new Teams & Members. Rather than letting Dagster materializing the 3 assets in a potentially arbitrary order I would prefer to have it happen 1) Member 2) Team 3) Event so that I know I'll always end up having the Team that every member belongs to and the Event that every Team is a part of.

```
@asset 
def events():
    # get events table data
    ...

@asset
def teams():
    # get teams table data
    ...

@asset
def team_members():
    # get team members table data
    ...
```


I suppose that I could just have Member be a dependency of Team and Team be a dependency of Event and then Dagster would materialize them in the desired order but that doesn't quite seem right. To my, admittedly very new, understanding of things, dependencies are more for what data you need to have in order to build a given downstream asset - and that's not exactly the relationship I have here.

What's the best approach for this?
* asset_sensors? That's for triggering additional jobs when an asset is materialized, so do I need to use define_asset_job to get my Team & Event assets as jobs first? Then the asset sensor waits for Member to materialize and triggers the Team job (and a second sensor triggers the Event job after)
* run_status_sensors (seems similar to the above since they rely on jobs not assets?)
* Just stick with making Member a dependency of Team and Team a dependency of Event and not worry about it?
* Some better way I've not thought of?

Thanks in advance!
questions.reference_answer:
> dependencies are more for what data you need to have in order to build a given downstream asset - and that's not exactly the relationship I have here.

Not necessarily- the `deps` parameter is agnostic as to whether data is or isn't being passed to the downstream asset. 

Your approach above using asset sensors is effectively hand-rolling the native functionality you would get if you defined the assets with dependency relationships. That would allow you to use [Declarative Automation](https://docs.dagster.io/guides/automate/declarative-automation/), which abstracts that all away.
questions.curate_reason: Chunks discuss asset sensors (user's option), declarative automation (recommended solution), and temporal dependencies without data passing (core concept).

questions.title: Problem with dependencies of dbt_assets
questions.reference_answer:
Just in case anyone has the same problem I have, I have achieved what I wanted. Instead of modifying the dagster code with any functionality, I have modified the dbt project. I have created a source with the dataflow dagster asset in the models folder (in a yml file) along with the other sources that previously existed as follows :

```
  - name: dagster
    tables:
      - name: import_filter_job__import_wf
        meta:
          dagster:
            asset_key: ["import_filter_job__import_wf"]
```


Then, I also added in the dbt model I wanted to depend on the dataflow job a line specifying that this models depends on that source as follows:

```
-- depends_on: {{ source('dagster','import_filter_job__import_wf') }}

SELECT ...
```

Before specifying it directly on the model I had tried modyfing the dbt_project.yml file to add that dependency on the whole folder that contains this model, but it didn't worked. I selected this concrete model to specify this dependency because I knew it was the one I knew the other models depended on.

questions.title: What to do about DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError?
questions.question:
What to do about DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError?

These errors can arise while launching a job:

```
dagster._core.errors.DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: Timed out waiting for call to user code GET_SUBSET_EXTERNAL_PIPELINE_RESULT [9f172afa-3dde-4cff-a4d9-a7526fbfd6cc]
```

or while evaluating sensors:

```
dagster._core.errors.DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: dagster._core.errors.DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: The sensor tick timed out due to taking longer than 60 seconds to execute the sensor function. One way to avoid this error is to break up the sensor work into chunks, using cursors to let subsequent sensor calls pick up where the previous call left off.

Stack Trace:
  File "/dagster-cloud/dagster_cloud/agent/dagster_cloud_agent.py", line 802, in _process_api_request
    api_result = self._handle_api_request(
  File "/dagster-cloud/dagster_cloud/agent/dagster_cloud_agent.py", line 662, in _handle_api_request
    serialized_sensor_data_or_error = client.external_sensor_execution(
  File "/dagster/dagster/_grpc/client.py", line 406, in external_sensor_execution
    chunks = list(
  File "/dagster/dagster/_grpc/client.py", line 194, in _streaming_query
    self._raise_grpc_exception(
  File "/dagster/dagster/_grpc/client.py", line 145, in _raise_grpc_exception
    raise DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError(

The above exception was caused by the following exception:
grpc._channel._MultiThreadedRendezvous: <_MultiThreadedRendezvous of RPC that terminated with:
	status = StatusCode.DEADLINE_EXCEEDED
	details = "Deadline Exceeded"
	debug_error_string = "UNKNOWN:Deadline Exceeded {created_time:"2023-11-04T20:49:29.061099977+00:00", grpc_status:4}"
>
```
questions.reference_answer:
Setting these timeout values is deployment-specific. If using a Kubernetes agent configured with Helm chart, ­[in the chart template](https://artifacthub.io/packages/helm/dagster-cloud/dagster-cloud-agent?modal=values) there is:

​`serverProcessStartupTimeout`​​ - Specifies the timeout in seconds the agent should use when waiting for a code server to be ready after the Kubernetes deployment is created. This timeout should be increased if your code locations can take more than 3 minutes to load your Dagster definitions.

This is the time required for the code location container to start and the grpc server being ready and serving requests. If you are using many libraries, or are creating a dbt manifest dynamically, you may need to increase this.

`​deploymentStartupTimeout​​` - Specifies the timeout in seconds when creating a new Kubernetes deployment for a code server. If not set, defaults to 300 seconds.

This is the time the agent will wait from the deployment initialization to the pod being running. If your cluster depends on slow node provisioning when starting new pods, you might need to increase that.

There is an extensive guide for debugging and managing sensor timeouts specifically in https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/20021
questions.curate_reason: The error is a concrete timeout problem, the accepted answer is substantive with specific configuration guidance, and the chunks directly address sensor timeout configuration (OSS and Dagster+) and code server startup timeout settings that resolve the scenarios shown.

questions.title: How to access the context variable in a graph_multi_asset
questions.question:
How to access the context variable in a graph_multi_asset

In Dagster, how does one access the 'Context' variable within a graph_multi_asset?

E.g., I have context running through the underlying ops/ assets.
But, then I need to do this:

" base_path = Path(context.partition_key)"

I can't do it though, because dagster errors with: " dagster._core.errors.DagsterInvalidDefinitionError: "context" is not a valid name in Dagster. It conflicts with a Dagster or python reserved keyword."

So How does one access context metadata (per partition) within the graph_multi_asset?

Thanks heaps!!
questions.reference_answer:
In Dagster, the `context` parameter is used to pass execution-time information to assets and ops. However, when defining a `graph_multi_asset`, you cannot directly use `context` as a parameter name because it is a reserved keyword in Dagster. This is why you are encountering the `DagsterInvalidDefinitionError`.

To access context metadata within a `graph_multi_asset`, you should use the parameters that are passed to the underlying ops or assets. These parameters can include the partition key or other context-related information that you need.

If you need to access the partition key within a `graph_multi_asset`, you would typically do so within the individual ops that make up the graph, rather than at the graph level itself. Each op can access the partition key through its context parameter, assuming the op is part of a partitioned asset.

Here's an example of how you might access the partition key within an op that is part of a `graph_multi_asset`:

```python
from dagster import op, graph

@op
def my_op(context):
    base_path = Path(context.partition_key)
    # ... your logic here ...

@graph
def my_graph():
    my_op()

# Then you would define a `graph_multi_asset` that includes `my_graph`
```

In this example, `my_op` is an op that accesses the partition key from its context. When you define a `graph_multi_asset`, you would include `my_graph`, which in turn includes `my_op`.

If you need to pass additional context metadata to the ops within a `graph_multi_asset`, you can do so by defining resources or configuration that the ops can access through their context.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [4] directly supports the answer's core recommendation by explaining how ops access context via a `context` parameter, which is the solution to accessing context within graph_multi_assets' underlying ops.

questions.question:
Start a backfill for all partitions from a sensor

Hey, I want to start a backfill for all partitions of a partitioned run with a sensor.

I saw #19457 and tried the code below:
```
@dg.asset_sensor(
    asset_key=dg.AssetKey("trigger_asset"),
    job=target_job,

    minimum_interval_seconds=30,
)
def backfill_sensor(
    context: dg.SensorEvaluationContext,
) -> Generator[dg.RunRequest, None, None]:
    all_partitons = partition_def.get_partition_keys()
    yield dg.RunRequest(
        run_key=context.cursor,
        tags={
            "dagster/asset_partition_range_start": all_partitons[0],
            "dagster/asset_partition_range_end": all_partitons[-1],
            "dagster/backfill": context.cursor,
        },
    )
```

Unfortunately, I encountered several issues with this approach:

1. A single run starts instead of a backfill containing several runs.
2. The asset raises a `DagsterTypeCheckDidNotPass` stating that it got a `dict` as an input instead of the type that the asset expects. I guess this is a direct result of the single run instead of multiple runs for each partition being launched.

Alternatively, I know that it's possible to do
```
@dg.asset_sensor(
    asset_key=dg.AssetKey("trigger_asset"),
    job=target_job,
    default_status=dg.DefaultSensorStatus.RUNNING,

)
def backfill_sensor(

) -> Generator[dg.RunRequest, None, None]:
    all_partitons = partition_def.get_partition_keys()
    for partition in all_partitions:
        yield dg.RunRequest(
            run_key=f"{context.cursor}-{partition}",
            tags={"dagster/partition": partition},

```

This is not what I want since it creates many different runs that are not grouped under a single backfill.

Is there a good way to start a single backfill for all partitions from a sensor?

questions.title: backfill_policy not working?
questions.question:
backfill_policy not working?

In our team we have some very large models which we'd like to be able to separate into chunks and do a rolling backfill of data across multiple jobs instead of a single long-running job.

What we'd like to achieve:
- Configure a partitioned asset at a sensible grain, eg. daily
- Be able to run a full historical backfill for said asset
- Have the historical backfill materialise a fixed number of partitions in a single step

In the [backfill docs](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/partitions-schedules-sensors/backfills) it references that you can specify a backfill strategy, and dagster provides out of the box:
- `BackfillPolicy.single_run` to configure backfills to run as a single job
- `BackfillPolicy.multi_run` which will break down the backfill into chunks of `max_partitions_per_run` partitions per run

The `multi_run` config sounds excatly like what we'd want to explore, as we could configure backfills to materialise "X days" worth of partitions per job.

However setting the `backfill_policy` on assets doesn't seem to have any effect. Whether single_run / multi_run, and independently of what `max_partitions_per_run` setting we configure, backfills seems to launch 1 run per partition. If single_run worked we could also chunk it up within the asset execution, but ideally would be able to have several runs with a scoped number of partitions.

Tested on dagster versions 1.5.4 and 1.5.9.
 
Not sure if `backfill_policy` is just broken, or if we need to configure anything else within our assets or instance to make this work? This is how the assets are configured:

 ```
PARTITION_DEFINITION = DailyPartitionsDefinition(
    start_date=datetime(2021, 11, 17),
    end_date=datetime(2024, 11, 14),
    timezone="Etc/UTC",
)

@asset(
    partitions_def=PARTITION_DEFINITION,
    group_name="spike_partitions",
    compute_kind="python",
    backfill_policy=BackfillPolicy.multi_run(max_partitions_per_run=15),
)
def test_standalone_asset(context):
    start, end = context.partition_time_window
    context.log.info(
        f"Running test_standalone_asset for partitions: [{start.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')} , {end.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}]"
    )


@asset(
    partitions_def=PARTITION_DEFINITION,
    group_name="spike_partitions",
    backfill_policy=BackfillPolicy.single_run(),
)
def test_standalone_asset_single_backfill(context):
    start, end = context.partition_time_window
    context.log.info(
        f"Running test_standalone_asset_single_backfill for partitions: [{start.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')} , {end.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}]"
    )

test_partitions_job = define_asset_job(
    name="test_partitions_job",
    selection=AssetSelection.groups("spike_partitions")
)
```
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @jPinhao-Rover - I believe you're hitting a current known limitation, which is that backfill policies don't apply to backfills launched from the job page. They should work if you launch the backfills from the asset graph or the asset details page.

Here are two relevant issues:
- https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/17404
- https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/17665
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [0] directly states the key limitation: backfill policies only apply when launched from the asset graph or asset page, not the job page, which explains why the user's configured policy has no effect.

questions.title: How to get all asset keys of assets that are in a particular group?
questions.question:
How to get all asset keys of assets that are in a particular group?

How can I fetch all the asset keys of assets that are in a particular group (have a `group_name` parameter set)?
questions.reference_answer:
There's a way to do that using [AssetSelection ](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/assets#dagster.AssetSelection)syntax:

```python
group_selection = AssetSelection.groups("some_group")
asset_keys = selection.resolve(context.repository_def.asset_graph)
```

You can also get the asset key of the most downstream asset within a group using [sinks](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/assets#dagster.AssetSelection.sinks). A sink asset is an asset that has no downstream dependencies within the asset selection. The sink asset can have downstream dependencies outside of the asset selection. 

```python
downstream_asset_in_group_selection = AssetSelection.groups("some­_group").sinks()
asset­_keys = selection.resolve(context.repository­_def.asset­_graph)
```

You can also get the asset key of the most upstream asset within a group using [roots()](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/assets#dagster.AssetSelection.roots). A root asset is an asset that has no upstream dependencies within the asset selection. The root asset can have downstream dependencies outside of the asset selection.

```python
upstream_asset_in_group_selection = AssetSelection.groups("some­_group").roots()
asset­_keys = selection.resolve(context.repository­_def.asset­_graph)
```
questions.curate_reason: Chunks [2], [4], [13], [14] demonstrate selecting assets within specific groups and using roots/sinks functions, which directly address the core capability of retrieving asset keys by group shown in the answer.

questions.title: AttributeError: module 'lib' has no attribute 'X509_V_FLAG_NOTIFY_POLICY' exception while deploying Dagster code
questions.question:
AttributeError: module 'lib' has no attribute 'X509_V_FLAG_NOTIFY_POLICY' exception while deploying Dagster code

My Dagster code that uses snowflake and/or botocore suddenly started to fail on import / on deployment, with an error like this:

```
     import snowflake.connector
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/snowflake/connector/__init__.py:19: in <module>
     from .connection import SnowflakeConnection
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/snowflake/connector/connection.py:37: in <module>
     from .auth import (
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/snowflake/connector/auth/__init__.py:7: in <module>
     from ._auth import Auth, get_public_key_fingerprint, get_token_from_private_key
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/snowflake/connector/auth/_auth.py:55: in <module>
     from ..network import (
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/snowflake/connector/network.py:21: in <module>
     import OpenSSL.SSL
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/OpenSSL/__init__.py:8: in <module>
     from OpenSSL import crypto, SSL
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/OpenSSL/crypto.py:1570: in <module>
     class X509StoreFlags(object):
 .tox/py311/lib/python3.11/site-packages/OpenSSL/crypto.py:1589: in X509StoreFlags
     NOTIFY_POLICY = _lib.X509_V_FLAG_NOTIFY_POLICY
 E   AttributeError: module 'lib' has no attribute 'X509_V_FLAG_NOTIFY_POLICY'
```

How can I get my Dagster deployment working again?
questions.reference_answer:
This is caused by an upstream issue in the snowflake-connector-python package: https://github.com/snowflakedb/snowflake-connector-python/issues/2109 allowing versions of the `PyOpenSSL` package that aren't actually supported by the package.

To fix it, you can add the following to the Python environment your code is running in, usually by editing your `setup.py` or `requirements.txt` or `pyproject.toml` file:

```
pyOpenSSL>=22.1.0
```

A workaround will be included in the next released version of `dagster-snowflake`, planned for the first week of December 2024.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [3] directly supports the solution by explaining how to add Python library dependencies to a Dagster project's package configuration files (setup.py, requirements.txt, pyproject.toml).

questions.title: How to incrementally update an asset without using partitions
questions.question:
How to incrementally update an asset without using partitions

I have a source table, and I have a derived table with a transformed version of each of the records in the source table.

I want to update the derived table on a schedule, and I want to do this in an incremental way, i.e. to avoid dropping and re-creating the entire table each time I do this. I also don't want to use partitions.  Instead, I have a way of finding out which source records have been added / removed / changed since the last run.  I want to update only these records in the derived table.

Question adapted from this Slack message: https://dagster.slack.com/archives/C01U954MEER/p1686258558582379
questions.reference_answer:
Basically, you can handle this with a vanilla Dagster asset. You're responsible for writing the logic that finds out what records in the source table have changed and updates the relevant records in the target table.

You generally shouldn't use IO managers when using this pattern.  The built-in IO managers assume that every time you materialize an asset, you're overwriting the full asset or a full partition of the asset.

```python
from dagster import asset

@asset
def my_derived_table(context) -> None:
    added_records, deleted_records, updated_records = find_changed_records()

    transformed_added_records = transform_records(added_records)
    transformed_updated_records = transform_records(updated_records)

    update_table(
        add_records=transformed_added_records
        update_records=transformed_updated_records,
        delete_records=deleted_records,
    )
```

To make it easy / possible to find out what records have changed, it can be useful to store metadata each time you materialize your asset.  E.g. you might store the timestamp of the last record you observed, or some sort of cursor into an update stream.

https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/14338 includes information on how to retrieve metadata from prior materializations of the same asset.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [1] directly supports the metadata storage recommendation for tracking changed records across materializations.

Is it possible to make Dagster work like an event-driven workflows?

Hello,

I apologize for posing this question, as it seems Dagster is not primarily designed for real-time event-driven workflows. However, after a brief month of use, Dagster has certainly captivated me with its comfort and ease of use. I am reluctant to leave it behind in search of another task orchestration framework.

If I record events into a database and set the sensor's minimum_interval_seconds to 1, checking the database for new events every second, would this be a dangerous setup?

Or if not using such frequent sensors, is there a very simple way to trigger a job from the outside? I'm not looking for the CLI, but rather, is there a WebHook or REST API that allows a scheduler to run a job?
Thank you for your time and assistance.
questions.reference_answer:
I don't think 1 second sensor evaluations would be feasible. Also [note from in the docs ](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/partitions-schedules-sensors/sensors#evaluation-interval) about evaluation intervals: 
> It's important to note that this interval represents a minimum interval between runs of the sensor and not the exact frequency the sensor runs. If you have a sensor that takes 30 seconds to complete, but the minimum_interval_seconds is 5 seconds, the fastest Dagster daemon will run the sensor is every 35 seconds. The minimum_interval_seconds only guarantees that the sensor is not evaluated more frequently than the given interval.

[The GraphQL API](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/webserver/graphql) can be used to launch job executions and re-executions, allowing users to trigger executions on custom events. Check out https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/21131 for examples.
questions.curate_reason: Chunks directly address sensor minimum_interval_seconds behavior and confirm Dagster's support for event-driven workflows.

questions.title: Auto-materialization for dbt views using code version
questions.question:
Auto-materialization for dbt views using code version

I'm wondering if it would be possible to build auto-materialize rules so that a a dbt view asset will only update if it's code version has changed.

As it stands, with an "eager" policy, every view will update anytime an upstream asset updates. However, if the view code has not changed, it's actually unnecessary to run a materialization, as it's not producing any changes to the data model.

This results in Dagster doing a bunch of extra work and a busier server, which has real costs if your running it in a kubernetes cluster. It would be great if we could specify that view models should be passed over if there has been no code change when evaluated.
questions.reference_answer:
This is now possible using the [Declarative Automation](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/automation/declarative-automation#declarative-automation). You can set the `automation_condition` for your dbt views to `AutomationCondition.code_version_changed()` to have them only materialize when a code version change is detected.

You can accomplish this by updating the `get_automation_condition()` method on your `DagsterDbtTranslator` to return this condition for dbt models which are views.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [0] describes code_version_changed() for dbt models; chunk [4] explains customizing automation conditions via DagsterDbtTranslator.get_automation_condition()

questions.title: Job Concurrency - When running, block all others. How?
questions.question:
Job Concurrency - When running, block all others. How?

All my jobs have a unique tag `{“single_run_per_job”: <job_name>}` which prevents the same job from running more than once concurrently. In my `dagster.yaml`

```yaml
tag_concurrency_limits:
      - key: single_run_per_job
        value:
          applyLimitPerUniqueValue: True
        limit: 1
```

What I would like to achieve is having a job that if it is running, no other job can be running as well.
Is this possible to achieve?

For example, I would like to have a job that performs optimize and vacuum to my tables, and I don't any other job running at the same time.
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @dinis-rodrigues, unfortunately this sort of concurrency limit is not supported in a convenient way at the moment -- all tag keys are treated equivalently, and so assuming there's only a single tag key (e.g. single_run_per_job), there's no way to have an instance of one specific job prevent all other jobs from running while still allowing any other combination of jobs to proceed in parallel.

Any solution here would need to have one distinct tag key per job, e.g. something like each job having `{f"{job_name}_concurrency_tag": True}`, and then the vacuum job would need to have all of those tags. This has obvious drawbacks, but is likely the only workaround at the moment.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk 5 documents an advanced method using schedules and metadata to prevent other runs from executing when one is in progress, directly addressing the user's goal of having one job block all others.

questions.reference_answer:
Hi @yamhirotoCS -- the `since()` condition is true if the top condition has become true more recently than the bottom condition.

questions.title: How to get code into deployment using a Dockerfile in a Dagster-dbt project deployed using helm/kubernetes
questions.question:
How to get code into deployment using a Dockerfile in a Dagster-dbt project deployed using helm/kubernetes

I am experimenting with Dagster to schedule my dbt pipeline. I already had a dbt project. I followed [the tutorial](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/dbt/using-dbt-with-dagster/load-dbt-models#step-1-create-a-dagster-project-that-wraps-your-dbt-project) to get a scaffolded Dagster project into my dbt folder. Now I want to deploy this to an existing Azure managed kubernetes cluster (AKS). I followed [this toturial](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/guides/kubernetes/deploying-with-helm#deploying-dagster-on-helm) to get my initial setup, which also works fine as long as I use the example project.
