Then I get stuck trying to get my own code into the deployment. I could use some guidance how to format the Dockerfile and the values.yaml. 

### Dockerfile
There doesn't seem to be much documentation on how to format the Dockerfile besides [this example](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/blob/master/python_modules/automation/automation/docker/images/user-code-example/Dockerfile). I updated it by adding the dependencies that I need for my code. Next I have to change the copy statement. I don't know what `build_cache/` directory is, I don't have it, and I can't find any information on it when I search, either on google, github, or slack 🤷. So I simply changed it to copy the entire Dagster folder. It builds without issues. My resulting Dockerfile is the following (my root folder is my dbt project folder and also contains the Dockerfile. The Dagster project is in `./dbt_test_dagster`): 

```Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

# All packages are hard-pinned to `dagster`, so setting the version on just `DAGSTER` will ensure
# compatible versions.
RUN pip install \
    dagster==1.5.10 \
    dagster-postgres \

    dagster-celery-k8s \
    dbt-duckdb \

# Get example pipelines
COPY ./dbt_test_dagster/ /
```

### Values.yml

```yaml
dagster-user-deployments:
```

Obviously, this doesn't work, but I don't know why. Upgrading helm with these overrides creates a pod `dagster-dagster-user-deployments-blablabla` which enters a `CrashLoopBackOff` cycle. When inspecting with `kubectl describe pod` it shows "successfully pulled image <image url>" after which it says "Back-off restarting failed container". I feel like both my Dockerfile and my values.yml are incorrect.

questions.title: Question about AutomationCondition.eager() conditions
questions.question:
Question about AutomationCondition.eager() conditions

Hey!

Using dagster version 1.8.5

This is the default eager AutomationCondition
```python
def eager() -> "AutomationCondition":
        with disable_dagster_warnings():
            return (
                AutomationCondition.in_latest_time_window()
                & (
                    AutomationCondition.newly_missing() | AutomationCondition.any_deps_updated()
                ).since_last_handled()
                & ~AutomationCondition.any_deps_missing()
                & ~AutomationCondition.any_deps_in_progress()
                & ~AutomationCondition.in_progress()
            ).with_label("eager")
```
I have a question about this condition specifically
```python
(
  AutomationCondition.newly_missing() | AutomationCondition.any_deps_updated()
).since_last_handled()
```
* Why `AutomationCondition.newly_missing().since_last_handled()` is used here and not just `AutomationCondition.missing()`. 

I guess there's some kind of an edge case but I can't figure it out. 

It would be great if you could provide me some context.
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @ssillaots-boku ! Good question, and the reason is indeed a bit of an edge case.

For simplicity, imagine you just have a single unpartitioned asset that has never been materialized, and has some error in its definition that will cause it to fail whenever the system attempts to materialize it.

In this case, the `eager()` condition would become true, and a run would be launched. While that run is going, no further work would be launched (as the asset would still be in progress). However, that run will eventually terminate with an error, at which point, the asset will still be missing, and no longer be in progress. This would cause another run to be kicked off, resulting in an "infinite loop" of errors.

The `newly_missing().since_last_handled()` structure avoids this by "remembering" that a run was already requested for this partition (since_last_handled is just shorthand for `since(updated() | requested())`.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [0] directly explains why newly_missing() is used instead of missing(): event-based conditions avoid continually requesting asset partitions that remain in the missing state across multiple evaluations.

questions.title: How to schedule a job with a partitioned asset?
questions.question:
How to schedule a job with a partitioned asset?

I want to schedule an asset job for a partitioned asset so that the partition for the then current day can be materialized.
questions.reference_answer:
There's a special purpose method for that: [build_schedule_from_partitioned_job](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/schedules-sensors#dagster.build_schedule_from_partitioned_job). There's a code example included in that documentation that I've copied below: 

```python
from dagster import (
    DailyPartitionsDefinition,
    asset,
    build_schedule_from_partitioned_job,
    define_asset_job,
    Definitions,
)

@asset(partitions_def=DailyPartitionsDefinition(start_date="2020-01-01"))
def asset1():
    ...

asset1_job = define_asset_job("asset1_job", selection=[asset1])

# The created schedule will fire daily
asset1_job_schedule = build_schedule_from_partitioned_job(asset1_job)

defs = Definitions(assets=[asset1], schedules=[asset1_job_schedule])
```

In the beloiw screenshot you can see when testing the schedule evaluation for a given day in the future that the `dagster/partition` tag will be for that day's date:

questions.title: Is using jinja2 for dynamically generating user code an antipattern?
questions.question:
Is using jinja2 for dynamically generating user code an antipattern?

Hi all,

I am using dagster for crawling/harvesting data from many URLs for constructing a knowledge graph
* I want each website to be able to be harvested individually if needed
* The extraction operation is standardized and the same for each website but 
**I don't know the list of websites to crawl until I get the list from an API call**


I am using jinja2 to dynamically generate each function decorated with `@ops`, `@jobs`, `@repository` etc
* This moves the code generation stage outside of dagster's pipelines
* This ends up giving me 3 python files for each source (i.e. one for the job, one for the op, and one for the schedule)
* However, it makes it easy to reason about since I have separate operations in the dagster UI, not just one big dynamic crawl.


I found this easy to reason about, but am unclear if it is optimal
*  There are lots of python files generated and code that is near duplicated in a scheduled step that is outside of dagster
* However, maybe this is ok since I want to be able to run the harvester for just one site at a time or all at once
    * This is the main reason I have kept it using templating. (I am still new to dagster and unclear  how else to do this)
* I want my solution to be scalable if I have hundreds of URLs

Thank you! (And if anyone has dagster projects that dynamically generate their own runtime code I would be very interested!)
questions.reference_answer:
Hi Colton, nice to meet you! Fellow Colton here -

Your approach is interesting, and does solve the problem, but I think there are a few considerations in which dynamic graphs may have some benefit, and I'd encourage you to explore them.

Like you said, your current approach is a two-step process. First, you generate the files, and then you can orchestrate them using Dagster. I believe this introduces an unnecessary overhead and forces the user to require additional maintenance of these files, when they could likely be generated dynamically in Dagster itself. You would have the same parameters that you are using to generate the files, however, they will be used as input to your dynamic graph of operations. There are three components to consider:

- *Scalability* - as you scale up your number of sources, you would end up with a ton of files
- *Maintainability* - you now have to consider keeping your generated files up-to-date, whether that be the template changing, or a new source being added. You also want to consider where you store those files, and how it may impact 
- *Flexibility* - with dynamic "parameterized" operations in Dagster, you can pass in parameters in the UI at runtime, for ad-hoc running of jobs. With the templated approach, this would be more difficult as you'd have to generate new files.

You may also be interested in the "factory-method" or domain specific language (DSL) approaches to building pipelines. With the former, you create a function that generates your DAG of operations. And with DSLs, you create an abstraction using something like YAML or JSON, that can then be read to create pipelines.

This factory-pattern guide is from our unreleased docs overhaul, but you may find it interesting!

- https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/23899/files
questions.curate_reason: Documents dynamic graphs and DSLs as recommended alternatives to jinja2 templating for generating operations at runtime with unknown parameters.

```python
defs = Definitions(
    ...,
    sensors=[
        AutomationConditionSensorDefinition(
            "­name",
            selection=AssetSelection.all(),
            run_tags={"dagster/max_runtime": 60},
        )
    ],
)
```

questions.title: How can I query my Dagster+ GraphQL API with my own custom query/mutation instead of using the DagsterGraphQLClient?
questions.question:
How can I query my Dagster+ GraphQL API with my own custom query/mutation instead of using the DagsterGraphQLClient?

The [DagsterGraphQLClient](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/webserver/graphql-client#graphql-python-client) doesn't currently support the query/mutation I want to send to my Dagster+ GraphQL API. How can I send my own custom query/mutation?
questions.reference_answer:
You can use the [gql library](https://pypi.org/project/gql/) to construct the query with your own custom mutation. 

```python
import os
from gql import Client, gql
from gql.transport.requests import RequestsHTTPTransport

# Define the endpoint URL and token
org_name = "your-org-name"
base_url = f"https://{org_name}.dagster.cloud/"
deployment_name = "prod" # string name for actual deployments, branch deployments use a string ID of their deploymentId
url = base_url + deployment_name + "/graphql"
token = os.getenv("DAGSTER_CLOUD_USER_TOKEN")  # a user token generated from the Organization Settings page in Dagster+. Note: use a user token, not agent token

# Define the transport with the endpoint URL and any headers if needed
transport = RequestsHTTPTransport(
    url=url,
    headers={
        "Dagster-Cloud-Api-Token": token,
    },
    use_json=True,
    timeout=120,
)

# Instantiate the client
client = Client(transport=transport)

# Define your custom GraphQL query/mutation. The below example fetches info about all your Dagster+ deployments
custom_query = gql("""
query {
  deployments {
    deploymentId
    deploymentName
    deploymentStatus
  }
}
""")

# Define the query variables values for the query/mutation, if any
query_variables = {
}

# Execute the query
try:
    result = client.execute(custom_query, variable_values=query_variables)
    print(result)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"An error occurred: {e}")
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete API usage question with substantive answer; chunk [0] directly demonstrates the gql library setup pattern for custom GraphQL queries to Dagster+ API.

questions.title: Run independent dbt selects for a @dbt_assets declaration with many models
questions.question:
Run independent dbt selects for a @dbt_assets declaration with many models

I'm wondering if there is a way to break up dbt run statement for `@dbt_assets` asset which selects many models.
Let me show an example.
```
@dbt_assets(manifest=manifest,
            select='some_model another_model last_model')
def daily_models(...):
    yield from dbt.cli(...)
```
So in Dagster this creates a run where dbt command would look like `dbt run  --select some_model another_model last_model`. So a singular run statement for all the models.
Now let's say that two first model run successfully but the last one fails. Then the run will retry and all the models will be ran again. It's fine if `@dbt_assets` doesn't select many assets. But let's say you have 20+ assets lined up where each run lasts for 3 minutes. So altogether ~60 minutes of runs. Now when the last run fails for some reason then we'll go again for another 60 minutes. In the end it gets costly as well (on Redshift's side).
I'm trying to find out if there's a way to break up singular dbt run statement into three separate dbt run statements. So that when a model run fails then the next Dagster run will pick off where it failed.
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @ssillaots-boku - When using dbt assets, the run launched by Dagster is leveraging a dbt CLI invocation with the `--select` flag, which means that a single  Dagster run selecting multiple dbt assets will always be invoked with a command like `dbt run --select some_model another_model last_model`.

To break your run statement in three, you'll need to create your own asset selections and jobs to launched individual Dagster runs. You can use schedules and sensors to orchestrate everything:

```python
@dbt_assets(manifest=manifest)
def my_dbt_assets(...):
    yield from dbt.cli(...)

# Create the asset selections and jobs
some_model_job = define_asset_job(
    name="some_model_job",
    selection=build_dbt_asset_selection(
        [my_dbt_assets],
        dbt_select="some_model"
    ),
)

another_model_job = define_asset_job(
    name="another_model_job",
    selection=build_dbt_asset_selection(
        [my_dbt_assets],
        dbt_select="another_model"
    ),
)

last_model_job = define_asset_job(
    name="last_model_job",
    selection=build_dbt_asset_selection(
        [my_dbt_assets],
        dbt_select="last_model"
    ),
)

# Create a daily schedule for `some_model`, the most upstream model
some_model_schedule = ScheduleDefinition(
    name="some_model_schedule",
    cron_schedule="@daily",
    job=some_model_job,
)

# Create a sensor for `another_model`
# If `some_model_job` succeeds, a run for `another_model_job` is launched
@run_status_sensor(
    run_status=DagsterRunStatus.SUCCESS,
    request_job=another_model_job,
    monitor_jobs=[some_model_job]
)
def another_model_sensor(context):
    yield RunRequest(...)
 
 
# Create a sensor for `last_model`
# If `another_model_job` succeeds, a run for `last_model_job` is launched
@run_status_sensor(
    run_status=DagsterRunStatus.SUCCESS,
    request_job=last_model_job,
    monitor_jobs=[another_model_job]
)
def last_model_sensor(context):
    yield RunRequest(...)
```

Note that in this pattern, you will launch N Dagster jobs, where N is the total number of dbt models that you want to select. 

If one specific model fails more often and you want to isolate it, but reduce the number of Dagster runs that are being launched, you can update your asset selections to group your dbt models:

```python
some_model_and_upstream_selection = build_dbt_asset_selection(
    [my_dbt_assets],
    dbt_select="some_model"
).upstream()

isolated_model_selection = build_dbt_asset_selection(
    [my_dbt_assets],
    dbt_select="isolated_model"
)

another_model_and_downstream_selection = build_dbt_asset_selection(
    [my_dbt_assets],
    dbt_select="another_model"
).downstream()
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete usage question about configuring dbt asset orchestration with a substantive answer demonstrating the build_dbt_asset_selection and job creation pattern.

questions.title: How to implement multiple production pathways for a single asset
questions.reference_answer:
Both upstream assets would have to materialize successfully for this structure to work. You can't materialize one upstream asset and not the other if they are both inputs or dependencies for a downstream asset. 

Depending on your use case, you could consider other approaches such as using [graph-backed assets](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/graph-backed-assets#graph-backed-assets) or using [multi-asset](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/multi-assets#multi-assets) with a resource as in this example:

```python
from dagster import resource, multi_asset, AssetOut, Output, Definitions, OpExecutionContext

@resource
def my_resource():
    def my_method():
        return "shared_value_from_my_resource"
    return my_method

@multi_asset(
    outs={
        "asset_1": AssetOut(is_required=False),
        "asset_2": AssetOut(is_required=False)
    },
    required_resource_keys={"my_resource"},
    can_subset=True
)
def my_assets_sharing_a_resource(context: OpExecutionContext):
    # all assets that will be output from this single function can share the value from the same resource
    shared_value = context.resources.my_resource()

    if "asset_1" in context.selected_output_names:
        # logic for defining the output of the asset named asset_1
        combined_output_1 = "asset_1 result: " + shared_value
        context.log.info(combined_output_1)
        yield Output(combined_output_1, output_name="asset_1")

    if "asset_2" in context.selected_output_names:
        # logic for defining the output of the asset named asset_2
        combined_output_2 = "asset_2 result: " + shared_value
        context.log.info(combined_output_2)
        yield Output(combined_output_2, output_name="asset_2")

defs = Definitions(
    assets=[my_assets_sharing_a_resource],
    resources={"my_resource": my_resource},
)
```
- Uses the can_subset and is_required options to make each asset able to be independently materialized if needed.
- These options can be omitted so that both assets always materialize together too.
- More info in: https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/multi-assets#subsetting-multi-assets
questions.curate_reason: The question is a concrete usage question, the answer substantively explains why the naive approach fails and provides working alternatives with code examples using multi-asset and subsetting features.

questions.title: Upstream dependencies for Automaterialization
questions.question:
Upstream dependencies for Automaterialization

Hi,
I have a question regarding AssetDeps. Currently, we are using them extensively, but we explicitly do not set some upstream dependencies because they are not as important as others. Imagine a mapping table that rarely changes. A downstream asset needs this table, but it is not required to be updated everyday. 
How do I set the Automaterialization rules for that? Is there a way to model the dependency and explicitly ignore certain upstream tables? 

This is similary to 'Auto-materialize even if some parents are missing' from the docs.

Cheers,
Matt
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @matthias-Q , this is now possible using Declarative Automation: https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/automation/declarative-automation/customizing-automation-conditions#targeting-dependencies

The simplest approach at the moment would be to start with copying [the implementation ](https://sourcegraph.com/search?q=repo:%5Egithub%5C.com/dagster-io/dagster%24+AutomationCondition+on_cron&patternType=keyword&sm=0) of `AutomationCondition.on_cron()`, as it seems like you want a daily cadence for your downstream asset. The behavior of this condition results in a run being launched for the downstream asset once per day, after _all_ of its parents have been updated during that day. This is problematic, as you don't care about updates for one of those upstreams.

To avoid this, you can tack a `.ignore(AssetSelection.keys(<tables you want to ignore>))` to the `all_deps_updated_since_cron` bit. This will allow the condition to avoid waiting for those tables to refresh. This would look like:

```python
            ...
            all_deps_updated_since_cron = AutomationCondition.all_deps_match(
                AutomationCondition.newly_updated().since(cron_tick_passed)
                | AutomationCondition.will_be_requested()
            ).ignore(AssetSelection.keys(...))
            ...
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete API question with a substantive answer; chunks [0], [5], and [12] directly support the solution of using `.ignore(AssetSelection.keys(...))` to exclude upstream dependencies from automaterialization conditions.

questions.title: Dagster+ Agent request timeouts
questions.question:
Dagster+ Agent request timeouts

# Symptoms

Requests from the Kubernetes Dagster+ agent are often timing out. This problem is indicated by the following error messages appearing frequently in the agent logs:

- `dagster_cloud_cli.core.errors.GraphQLStorageError:
HTTPSConnectionPool(host='{organisation}.agent.dagster.cloud', port=443):
Read timed out. (read timeout=60)`
- `requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='{organisation}.agent.dagster.cloud', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=60)`
- `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError: ('Connection aborted.', ConnectionResetError(104, 'Connection reset by peer'))`

# Possible causes

It is largely encountered in Kubernetes hybrid deployments where the network egress must traverse a NAT gateway or other proxies that may impose a timeout on the connection or where connections are often dropped between the agent and the Dagster+ API endpoints.

This can also happen due to known issues such as [port or IP exhaustion on platforms such as GCP CloudNAT](https://cloud.google.com/knowledge/kb/cloud-nat-is-dropping-or-limiting-egress-connectivity-000004263).
questions.reference_answer:
## Solutions

### TCP KEEPALIVE

When using the [dagster-cloud-agent helm charts](https://artifacthub.io/packages/helm/dagster-cloud/dagster-cloud-agent) with a version ≥1.7.3, you can use the following options to enable probing of TCP connections to preemptively detect broken connections and reduce or eliminate the problem.

Note that setting these options also requires the dagster version for all of your code locations to also be 1.7.3 or later.

```yaml
dagsterCloud:
  socketOptions:
    - ["SOL_SOCKET", "SO_KEEPALIVE", 1]
    - ["IPPROTO_TCP", "TCP_KEEPIDLE", 11]
    - ["IPPROTO_TCP", "TCP_KEEPINTVL", 7]
    - ["IPPROTO_TCP", "TCP_KEEPCNT", 5]
```

These values are relatively aggressive and will cause additional network packets to be set to probe and maintain the connection. You may want to experiment with these values and see what works for your needs.

How this works:

* [TCP keepalive overview](https://tldp.org/HOWTO/TCP-Keepalive-HOWTO/overview.html)

### Upgrade the Dagster+ agent and user code to 1.5.9+

Since 1.5.9, most of the calls made by the agent are now being retried, which makes the agent more resilient to networking errors.

### Script to reproduce and test networking changes

Running this script in their agent pod to attempt to reproduce (and test networking changes). 
Note: High concurrency can trigger issues such as Cloud NAT port exhaustion. Adjust and interpret results with care.

```python
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed

import requests
from dagster import DagsterInstance
try:
    from dagster_cloud_cli.core.graphql_client import (
        create_agent_graphql_client,
    )
except ImportError:
    from dagster_cloud_cli.core.graphql_client import (
        create_proxy_client as create_agent_graphql_client,
    )

# Script that attempts to reproduce network issues connecting to Dagster Cloud
# servers under load

NUM_TRIALS = 1000
CONCURRENCY = 5

di = DagsterInstance.get()

session = requests.Session()

# Disable retries for the purpose of the test
modified_client = create_agent_graphql_client(
    session,
    di.dagster_cloud_graphql_url,
    {**di._dagster_cloud_api_config, "retries": 0},
)


def _fetch():
    """Execute GraphQL query in a threadpool"""
    return modified_client.execute("query TestScriptQuery {__typename}")


with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=CONCURRENCY) as executor:
    count = 0
    finished = 0
    for f in as_completed(executor.submit(_fetch) for i in range(NUM_TRIALS)):
        finished = finished + 1
        print("Finished trial " + str(finished))
        if f.result():
            count += 1

print(f"{count} successful results")
```

From https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/dbt/reference#loading-dbt-models-from-a-dbt-project:

> When deploying your Dagster project to production, we recommend generating the manifest at build time to avoid the overhead of recompiling your dbt project every time your Dagster code is executed. A manifest.json should be precompiled and included in the Python package for your Dagster code.

questions.title: How to organize single Dagster repo with multiple code locations and DBT projects
questions.question:
How to organize single Dagster repo with multiple code locations and DBT projects

![image](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/assets/6832968/17bdbdb2-9e6a-4ba7-9d7c-8d83e63faf61)

I have a single Git repository which contains multiple code locations. Each code location represents the assets for one of our clients. Each client requires assets derived from both Python code and DBT code. I expect the amount of DBT models to be quite low, couple of dozen at most.

My current setup is depicted in Option 1: separate DBT repo for each client. Until now I've been doing a proof-of-concept on a single client, and reproducing the set up to another client has felt quite heavyweight, and prone to misconfiguration. 

I'm considering option 2, by which I have a single DBT project that contains all the DBT models required for all clients, and each code location pulls in the DBT models it requires through appropriate use of the `@dbt_assets` decorator and `select/exclude`.

Is their precedent for either option? I'm leaning towards Option 2, whilst it breaks encapsulation it would be less set up for onboarding new clients. 

The reason each client has their own code location is to enable better separation of their assets in the UI (as code locations give a another level of grouping), it's possible that UI modifications may make this point moot, as I'd be satisfied with everything in a single code location if the UI would facilitate it - https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/16657#discussioncomment-7622296
questions.reference_answer:
Hi Steven,

Right now there are roughly two levels of grouping, as you point out: code locations, which define different execution environments & also act as collapsible sets of definitions in the UI, and asset groups which show up in the asset graph as collapsible.

The second option seems reasonable to me, especially if you limit the set of assets you're loading in each code location with selection.  We use this pattern internally where DBT models corresponding to different use-cases are colocated in the same DBT project. You could [use a custom DBT translator or metadata to assign asset groups](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/dbt/reference#customizing-group-names) which could let you subdivide by client or function.

We've been exploring concepts for nested groups, but don't yet have a concrete proposal/roadmap on this end.
questions.curate_reason: Question asks how to organize multiple code locations with DBT assets; the accepted answer recommends using a custom DBT translator to assign asset groups by client/function, which is directly addressed by documentation chunks on customizing group names.

questions.title: Serverless deployment code location errors: DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: Could not reach user code server. gRPC Error code: UNAVAILABLE
questions.question:
Serverless deployment code location errors: DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: Could not reach user code server. gRPC Error code: UNAVAILABLE

# Symptoms

Specifically for Dagster+ Serverless deployments, attempting to add a new code location, or redeploy an existing code location, results in errors like: 

- `dagster.­_core.errors.DagsterUserCodeUnreachableError: Could not reach user code server. gRPC Error code: UNAVAILABLE`

# Possible causes

- Typically these errors are caused due to user code taking too long to load, or a call within the user code hanging such as to an external API. 
- In some other instances, the cause is [Serverless compute and memory resource limits](https://docs.dagster.io/dagster-plus/deployment/serverless#limitations) being exceeded.
questions.reference_answer:
## Solutions

- Confirm long code load time and timeout by running Dagster locally
- If your project is using dbt assets, something to check is if your code is generating a dbt manifest at run time, rather than at the recommended build time. See more in: [Loading dbt models from a dbt project](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/dbt/reference#loading-dbt-models-from-a-dbt-project)
- Sometimes the root cause is sensor evaluations constantly timing out. See more in: https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/20021
- More generally, we have a guide showing [how to profile your code using py-spy](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/14771). Another utility that is useful is [memray](https://github.com/bloomberg/memray)
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [2] directly explains gRPC timeout behavior and code loading delays that cause UNAVAILABLE errors; chunk [3] is the py-spy profiling guide explicitly mentioned in the accepted answer.

questions.title: For production deployments, is it generally okay to have more than one dagster daemon instance?
questions.question:
For production deployments, is it generally okay to have more than one dagster daemon instance?

Quick question: For production deployments, is it generally okay to have more than one dagster daemon instance? From what I understand, they're used to kickoff various background tasks like sensors and auto-materialization of assets. What safeguards would be in place to handle race conditions between multiple dagster daemon instances such as two of them picking up the same task?

---

*The question was originally asked in [Dagster Slack](https://dagster.slack.com/archives/C01U954MEER/p1698780432004019?thread_ts=1698780432.004019&cid=C01U954MEER).*
questions.reference_answer:
No. "A deployment can have multiple simultaneous instances of dagster-webserver, but should include only a single dagster-daemon process." From [Running the Dagster daemon](https://docs.dagster.io/deployment/guides/service#running-the-dagster-daemon) documentation.

Having multiple instances of the `dagster-daemon` could lead to race conditions and other issues.

questions.title: How do I connect to a database that requires SSH tunneling, especially for dbt?
questions.question:
How do I connect to a database that requires SSH tunneling, especially for dbt?

My database/data warehouse requires an SSH tunnel to connect via port forwarding.

How do I connect to it when using Dagster? My use case is primarily for running transformations for dbt, if that context helps.
questions.reference_answer:
This will depend on how you're creating the SSH tunnel. For example, if you're using `sshtunnel`, then you can use this snippet to generate make wrap around the `asset` decorator to make your `ssh_asset` decorator, like below:

```python
def ssh_asset(fn):

    @asset
    def _ssh_asset():
        server = SSHTunnelForwarder(
            ('ssh.somewhere.com'),
            ssh_username='username',
            ssh_password='password',
            remote_bind_address=('services.com', 8080)
        )
        
        server.start()
        fn()
        server.stop()

    return _ssh_asset

@ssh_asset
def connected_asset():
   ...
   # This asset is connected to the database via port forwarding
```

When using dbt, here's an example of how you can connect while using the `dbt_assets` API:

```python
from sshtunnel import SSHTunnelForwarder

@dbt_assets(manifest=dbt_manifest_path)
def my_dbt_project(context: OpExecutionContext, dbt: DbtCliResource):
    server = SSHTunnelForwarder(
        'my.host.url.com',
        ssh_username="username",
        ssh_password="password",
        remote_bind_address=('127.0.0.1', 8080)
    )
    tunnel.start()

    yield from dbt.cli(["build"], context=context).stream()

    tunnel.stop()
```
questions.curate_reason: This is a concrete configuration question with a substantive code-based answer; chunk [8] is Dagster's SSH integration documentation which directly addresses SSH connectivity for database connections.

questions.title: How to handle and materialize dbt sources
questions.question:
How to handle and materialize dbt sources

### Overview

Dagster currently handles dbt sources as external assets, but there are scenarios where treating them as materializable assets can provide advantages. Specifically, dbt sources can be materialized using the [dbt-external-tables](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-external-tables) package to create external tables (e.g., in BigQuery). This discussion should help finding potential advantages and disadvantages of the proposed solution.

### Key Points

1. **Materializable Assets for dbt Sources**
    - dbt sources should have the option to be handled as materializable assets, not just external assets.
    - The `dbt-external-tables` package allows the creation of dbt sources as external tables, such as in BigQuery:  `dbt run-operation stage_external_sources`
    
    This could resolve the following discussion:
    
    - https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/21457
2. **Loading Descriptions and Metadata for dbt Source Assets**
    - dbt sources, when loaded as “normal” assets, should be able to load descriptions and metadata similar to assets based on dbt models.
    - This will ensure consistency and completeness of metadata across all dbt assets in Dagster.
    
    This could resolve the following discussion:
    
    - https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/16470
3. **Mapping Upstream Dependencies for dbt Source Assets**
    - A new dbt [asset definition attribute](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/dbt/reference#understanding-asset-definition-attributes) can be implemented to map upstream dependencies to the asset that stores the raw files in an object storage (e.g., Parquet files in Google Cloud Storage).
    - **Example Attribute:** Using the dbt [meta config](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/resource-configs/meta) to add a dagster key, e.g. `deps`.
    
    This could resolve the following discussion:
    
    - https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/21077

### Example Configuration

Here's an example YAML configuration for a dbt source handled as a materializable asset with upstream dependency mapping:

```yaml
---

version: 2

sources:
  - name: example_bq_dataset
    tables:
      - name: src__table
        description: "External Delta Lake table stored as Parquet files in Google Cloud Storage"
        external:
          location: gs://bucket/delta-lake-table
          options:
            format: DELTA_LAKE
        meta:
          dagster:
            asset_key: ["gcp-project","example_bq_dataset","src__table"]
            group: "dbt_source"
            deps: ["gcp-project","bucket","delta-lake-table"]  # references the upstream asset that creates the delta lake table

```

Here’s an example Dagster implementation for a dbt source executing the `dbt.cli` method with `run-operation stage_external_sources` as input.

```yaml
@dbt_assets(
    manifest=manifest,
    select=DBT_SELECT_SOURCE,
    dagster_dbt_translator=CustomDbtTranslator(),
)
def dbt_source_assets(context: OpExecutionContext):
    yield from DBT.cli(["run-operation", "stage_external_sources"], manifest=manifest, context=context).stream()
```

### Conclusion

Is this approach viable and aligns with the core concepts of Dagster and dbt integration?

I appreciate any feedback on this matter.

P.S. if a contribution is welcomed, I can help there as well.
questions.reference_answer:
You can already what you want using the existing APIs. You could just read the sources from the manifest and define a function for what you want.

You can adapt a variation of https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/21626#discussioncomment-9306374.

1. Build a set of [`AssetSpec`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/assets#dagster.AssetSpec) by reading the manifest for dbt sources.
2. Pass those asset specs to a `@multi_asset` decorator.
3. Define the compute function to materialize those asset specs.

You can customize (1) to add upstream deps. For example, you could also make it read from the dbt source meta.

(2/3) allow you to define any computation to materialize (1). You're not constrained to `dbt run-operation`.
questions.curate_reason: This is a concrete how-to question with a substantive answer; chunks [5,10,11] directly support the answer by explaining how dbt sources are defined as assets, configured with metadata, and connected to upstream dependencies.

questions.title: How to programmatically check the last time an asset was materialized
questions.question:
How to programmatically check the last time an asset was materialized

How do I check the last time an asset was materialized in code?
questions.reference_answer:
You can do that by querying the `instance` via the `context`:

```python
from dagster import asset, AssetKey

@asset
def materialization_event_time(context):
    timestamp = context.instance.get_latest_materialization_event(asset_key=AssetKey("foo")).timestamp
    ...
```

You can also query for multiple assets:

```python
from dagster import asset, AssetKey

@asset 
def materialization_event_time(context):
    #asset_events will be a dictionary mapping AssetKey to event
    asset_events = context.instance.get_latest_materialization_events(asset_keys=[AssetKey("foo"), AssetKey("bar")])

    materialization_timestamps = []
    for event in asset_events.values():
        materialization_timestamps.append(event.timestamp)
    ...
```

Update:

I've managed to identify the root cause of the issue. It appears that I have a sensor running for a significantly longer duration, well beyond 60 seconds, in the deployment environment compared to the local development environment. Consequently, the Dagster daemon throws a gRPC client timeout error, while the gRPC server continues the evaluation. Considering this, would it be advisable to terminate gRPC calls that exceed the server-side timeout?

questions.title: What types of metadata can I add to a Dagster asset?
questions.question:
What types of metadata can I add to a Dagster asset?

Many dagster examples show asset metadata like the asset owner, the number of rows or execution time plotted, or the table schema. How can I add these values for my own asset?
questions.reference_answer:
Dagster allows you to attach rich metadata in a variety of places, see this [discussion](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/12350).

A common approach is to add metadata to the asset materialization. Here is an example:

```python
from dagster import asset, MetadataValue, AssetExecutionContext
import random 
import pandas as pd
from dagster_pandas.data_frame import create_table_schema_metadata_from_dataframe


@asset
def my_asset(context: AssetExecutionContext):
    """ the asset description """

    data = pd.DataFrame({
        "orders": [1,2,3,4],
        "users": ["pam", "jim", "michael", "dwight"]
    })

    context.add_output_metadata({
        "numeric_value": random.choice(range(1,100)),
        "key": "value",
        "tabular_data": MetadataValue.md(data.head().to_markdown()),
        "table_schema": create_table_schema_metadata_from_dataframe(data)
    })

    return data
```

Here is the result:

![Screen Shot 2023-07-20 at 8 28 06 AM](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/assets/15901072/c08aed94-f62b-4964-a22a-0515e1d35aa0)

Note that numeric metadata will automatically be plotted by materialization.


Another example from our quickstart project includes adding an image: https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/blob/master/examples/quickstart_etl/quickstart_etl/assets/hackernews.py#L67-L81


Other examples and guides from the docs:
- https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/assets/software-defined-assets#recording-materialization-metadata
- https://docs.dagster.io/guides/dagster/managing-ml#enhancing-the-dagster-ui-with-metadata
- https://docs.dagster.io/tutorial/building-an-asset-graph#step-3-educating-users-with-metadata
questions.curate_reason: This is a concrete API/usage question with a substantive, code-based answer; chunks [6,5,8] directly list the types of metadata attachable to assets (ownership, tags, rich information, table schema, etc.), and [2] explains the runtime metadata attachment approach.

questions.title: How can we build a scheduled job with dbt assets from two different databases based on dbt tags?
questions.question:
How can we build a scheduled job with dbt assets from two different databases based on dbt tags?

Hello Folks,

I have two different dbt assets with tag 'XXX'. One of my asset is in Sql Server other one is in IBM DB2.
I am trying to merge these two asset group in one single module and then trying to create a dbt asset schedule with build_schedule_from_dbt_selection depending on tag 'XXX'. My code look like this:

**__init__.py :**
all_assets = load_assets_from_modules([assets_SS, assets_DB2], key_prefix='ll')

daily_dbt_assets_schedule = build_schedule_from_dbt_selection(
    all_assets ,
    job_name="all_dbt_assets",
    cron_schedule="0 0 * * *",
    dbt_select="tag:XXX",
)

defs = Definitions(
    assets=all_assets,
    .
    .
    .
    ) 

Where assets_SS and assets_DB2 are two different python files containing only simple **@dbt_assets** function. (I am just loading my assets through manifest.)

Normally I have lot of active dagster-dbt jobs both on Sql Server and IBM DB2 but when I run my code with the example above I get an error like this:
**dagster._check.CheckError: Invariant failed. Description: Exactly one dbt AssetsDefinition is required** 

Am I trying to do something is not supported? How can I achive my goal?
Thank you for your time.
questions.reference_answer:
[`build_dbt_asset_selection`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/libraries/dagster-dbt#dagster_dbt.build_dbt_asset_selection) and [`build_schedule_from_dbt_selection`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/libraries/dagster-dbt#dagster_dbt.build_schedule_from_dbt_selection) are convenience APIs for creating a [`DbtAssetSelection`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/libraries/dagster-dbt#dagster_dbt.DbtManifestAssetSelection) or [`ScheduleDefinition`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/schedules-sensors#dagster.ScheduleDefinition) from a single [`@dbt_assets`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/libraries/dagster-dbt#dagster_dbt.dbt_assets) definition.

If you have multiple dbt assets definitions, then we recommend that you just construct `DbtAssetSelection` or `ScheduleDefinition` directly.

For example:

```python
from dagster import ScheduleDefinition, define_asset_job
from dagster_dbt import DbtManifestAssetSelection

manifest = ...

my_schedule = ScheduleDefinition(
    name="all_dbt_assets_daily_schedule",
    cron_schedule="0 0 * * *",
    job=define_asset_job(
        name="all_dbt_assets",
        selection=DbtManifestAssetSelection.build(
            manifest=manifest,
            select="tag:XXX",
        ),
    ),
)
```
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [2] directly lists DbtManifestAssetSelection (the recommended API for this case), and chunk [1] demonstrates the pattern of manually constructing schedules with asset selections when convenience functions don't apply.

questions.title: How to run different dbt commands for the same assets
questions.question:
How to run different dbt commands for the same assets

Sorry, not entirely sure how to word this, so perhaps an example better illustrates this.

If I'm not mistaken, the asset definitions via the `dbt_assets` decorator couples the dbt commands and the asset definitions. This make it awkward to say run a full refresh every so often.

Now, I've seen in the documentation this example:
```python
from pathlib import Path

from dagster import AssetExecutionContext, Config
from dagster_dbt import DbtCliResource, dbt_assets

class MyDbtConfig(Config):
    full_refresh: bool

@dbt_assets(manifest=Path("target", "manifest.json"))
def data_dbt_assets(context: AssetExecutionContext, dbt: DbtCliResource, config: MyDbtConfig):
    dbt_build_args = ["build"]
    if config.full_refresh:
        dbt_build_args += ["--full-refresh"]

    yield from dbt.cli(dbt_build_args, context=context).stream()
```

I assume this does the job when running jobs in the UI, however, I can't workout how I can schedule two jobs that act on the same set of assets, but:
* One runs `dbt run`
* The other runs `dbt run --full-refresh`

These models are simply partitioned, i.e. the cursor is derived from the underlying data, not passed as a parameter--I also don't intend to change this for now as it would take considerable time.

One solution, which I dislike, was to bake into the asset definition (the function decorated with the `dbt_assets` decorator) some conditional that checks the day of the week. This is bad in the sense that if anyone wants to run a job that day, it is _always_ full refresh.

What I really need, is some ability to configure a job. The job can run the _same_ asset selection, but if some context can be passed through to the asset definition, then we could append the `--full-refresh` to build commands. However, I can't seem to find anything about configuring jobs.

Any help would be appreciated as this feels like a blocker in using the integration at present. Thanks.
questions.reference_answer:
When using [`define_asset_job`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/assets#dagster.define_asset_job) to define a job for a set of assets, you can pass a [`RunConfig`](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/config#dagster.RunConfig) to the `config` that lets you specify the `MyDbtConfig` that you want when the job runs.

See a related discussion in https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/18803#discussioncomment-7899794.
questions.curate_reason: This is a concrete configuration question with a substantive answer; chunks 0, 2, and 5 directly support using RunConfig to configure dbt jobs with different command flags like --full-refresh.

questions.title: Run daily partitions more frequently than once a day
questions.question:
Run daily partitions more frequently than once a day

A common use case in data engineering is to have data that needs to be updated very frequently for a given day but otherwise can be represented less granularly. For example, imagine you have a pipeline that pulls product order data from an upstream service. Each order is represented as a file. You may want to get all the new order data every 5 minutes during the current day. What is the best way to represent this pipeline so that it is observable and actionable?

One option would be to represent each order as a partition in Dagster. However,  Dagster's partition system is optimized for O(1000s) of partitions, so if your system has millions of orders, you will quickly surpass this limit. 

Another option would be to create a partitioning scheme that matches your data SLA, e.g., a partition for every 5 minutes. This would be easy to schedule in Dagster and allow a human to understand the state of your incremental data processing throughout the day. However, a 5-minute interval would still quickly surpass Dagster's partition limit. This granularity could also make backfills a challenge, and it is unlikely the 5-minute granularity would be useful for historic days. 

Instead, we recommend a middle-ground: a daily partition scheme where the current day is updated multiple times throughout the day. How is this accomplished?

Keywords for search engines: run daily partitioned asset on hourly schedule
questions.reference_answer: The key to this type of pipeline is making sure your asset functions are safe to run the same partition multiple times. Here is example code showing a potential structure: ```python from dagster import ( DailyPartitionsDefinition, asset, AssetExecutionContext, ScheduleEvaluationContext, BackfillPolicy, schedule, define_asset_job, AssetSelection, RunRequest, Definitions, ) import random def remove_partial_day(day, context): """Given a date, removes files for this day from s3 if they exist""" # for example context.log.info(f"Found {random.randint(0,10)} existing files for {day} to delete") ... def ftp_to_s3(day, context): """Gets files from ftp for a day and writes them to s3""" # for example context.log.info(f"Getting {random.randint(0,10)} files for {day}") ... def clickhouse_remove_day(day, context): """Drop records for a given day to avoid writing duplicates""" # for example context.log.info(f"Dropping existing records, if any, for {day}") ... def clickhouse_load_day(day, context): """Load all records for a given day""" # for example context.log.info(f"Loading records for {day}") ... # supply an end_offset of one so we can update the _current day's_ partition throughout the day daily_partitions_with_offset = DailyPartitionsDefinition( start_date="2023-01-01", end_offset=1 ) @asset( partitions_def=daily_partitions_with_offset, backfill_policy=BackfillPolicy.multi_run(), # this asset does not know how to process multiple days within one run, so a multi run backfill policy is appropriate ) def ftp_source_daily(context: AssetExecutionContext): """Get all files from a ftp source for a day, load to s3, safe to run multiple times a day""" day_to_process = context.partition_key remove_partial_day(day_to_process, context) ftp_to_s3(day_to_process, context) return @asset( deps=[ftp_source_daily], partitions_def=daily_partitions_with_offset, backfill_policy=BackfillPolicy.multi_run(), # this asset does not know how to process multiple days within one run, so a multi run backfill policy is appropriate ) def clickhouse_daily(context: AssetExecutionContext): """Load all data for a day, safe to run multiple times a day""" day_to_process = context.partition_key # First drop all existing records for this partition clickhouse_remove_day(day_to_process, context) # Then load all data for this day clickhouse_load_day(day_to_process, context) return # create a job targeting our daily assets for our schedule to use load_data = define_asset_job( name="load_data", selection=AssetSelection.assets(clickhouse_daily).upstream() ) @schedule(job=load_data, cron_schedule="*/5 * * * *") def update_frequently(context: ScheduleEvaluationContext): """Update our daily assets as frequently as every 5 minutes""" day_to_process = context.scheduled_execution_time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d") return RunRequest(run_key=None, partition_key=day_to_process) # optional defs = Definitions( assets=[clickhouse_daily, ftp_source_daily], schedules=[update_frequently], jobs=[load_data], ) ``` To run this sample in Dagster you can execute `dagster dev -f definitions.py`. The regularly scheduled job will update the current day's partition multiple times throughout a given day. This will appear as multiple materialization events for the same partition: The schedule frequency (every 5 minutes) will dictate how quickly new data is processed and available. The historical data can still be backfilled at the _daily_ granularity: This example code has assets that only know how to process one day at a time so a `BackfillPolicy.multi_run( )` is used, meaning a backfill will launch a run per day. Alternatively, you could write assets to process a range of dates. The main downside to this approach is that if one order fails, it will fail the entire _day_. Logging within the processing functions can help the data engineer identify which order failed for a given day.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk 10 directly explains the end_offset parameter and how positive values enable filling the current day's partition, which is the core mechanism in the answer.

questions.title: partitioned asset downstream of non-partitioned asset
questions.question:
partitioned asset downstream of non-partitioned asset

What is the expected behavior when I have a monthly partitioned asset downstream of a non-partitioned daily asset?

The purpose of the non-partitioned daily asset is to ensure that the data that the monthly partitioned asset relies on is up to date.

Will the monthly partitioned asset wait for the daily non-partitioned asset to run (desired behavior), or is there a chance that the monthly partitioned asset sees yesterdays materialization of the daily non-partitioned asset and kicks off, since the daily asset is non-partitioned (undesirable behavior).

I'm using automaterialization.

The flow is essentially:

1. automaterialize daily non-partitioned root asset with cron rule
2. other downstream daily non-partitioned assets kick off with eager automaterialize policy
3. monthly partitioned assets kick off once steps 1 and 2 are done

Thanks!
questions.reference_answer:
The expected behavior is that the monthly partitioned asset will wait for the daily non-partitioned asset to run before it kicks off. This is because the auto-materialization system in Dagster is designed to ensure that downstream assets are only materialized when their upstream dependencies are up to date.

In your case, you have a non-partitioned daily asset that is set to auto-materialize with a cron rule. This means that the asset will be materialized based on the schedule defined by the cron expression. Downstream non-partitioned assets with an eager auto-materialize policy will then attempt to materialize as soon as their upstream dependencies (including the daily non-partitioned root asset) have been materialized.

For the monthly partitioned assets, the behavior will depend on the auto-materialize policy you have set for them. If they also have an eager auto-materialize policy, they will wait until all their upstream dependencies, including the daily non-partitioned assets, have been materialized for the current period before attempting to materialize.

By default, the eager policy will only materialize an asset if it's missing or one of its parents update. This means that an unpartitioned root asset will only get auto-materialized a single time, as it has no parents which can update. However, you can use the `materialize_on_cron` rule to ensure that the root asset is re-materialized on a regular basis according to a cron schedule.

Here's an example from the documentation that shows how to set up a `materialize_on_cron` policy:

```python
from dagster import AutoMaterializePolicy, AutoMaterializeRule, asset

materialize_on_cron_policy = AutoMaterializePolicy.eager().with_rules(
    AutoMaterializeRule.materialize_on_cron("0 9 * * *", timezone="US/Central"),
)

@asset(auto_materialize_policy=materialize_on_cron_policy)
def root_asset():
    ...
```

In this example, the `root_asset` will be materialized every day at 9 AM Central Time. If your monthly partitioned assets depend on this `root_asset`, they will only materialize once the `root_asset` has been updated according to its cron schedule.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [1] directly supports the core answer that automation conditions will wait for all upstream dependencies to be updated before executing downstream assets.

questions.title: Customize monthly partition
questions.question:
Customize monthly partition

I have a situation where I need to customize a monthly partitioned asset as follows:

- the earliest "start_date" of the data will be the first of the month
- the latest "start_date" of the data will be the last of the month
- The latest "end_date" of the data will be three days after the end of the month

Another way to describe this is looking at events (rows) that occurred during a month, and any related follow up events (rows) that happened during the month and up until three days after the end of the month.

I would thus want to run this asset on the fourth of the month, and it would look at data bounded by the "start_date" (first and last of month), with "end_date" potentially and likely going up until the third of the new month.

Is this feasible in dagster? Thanks!
questions.reference_answer:
You can use the `MonthlyPartitionsDefinition` class provided by Dagster, and then customize the asset computation to filter data based on the specific time bounds you need.

How you might set this up:

1. Define your monthly partitions with a `MonthlyPartitionsDefinition` that starts on the first of the month.

2. Schedule the asset to run on the fourth of the month. This can be done using a sensor or a schedule that triggers the materialization of the asset.

3. Within the asset computation, use the `context.partition_time_window` to get the start and end bounds of the partition. Then, adjust the end bound to be three days after the end of the month.

Here's an example of how you might define such an asset:

```python
from dagster import asset, MonthlyPartitionsDefinition, AssetExecutionContext
import pandas as pd
from datetime import timedelta

# Define your monthly partitions starting from a specific date
monthly_partition_def = MonthlyPartitionsDefinition(start_date="2023-01-01")

@asset(partitions_def=monthly_partition_def)
def my_monthly_asset(context: AssetExecutionContext) -> pd.DataFrame:
    # Get the time window for the current partition
    bounds = context.partition_time_window
    start_date = bounds.start
    # Adjust the end date to be three days after the end of the month
    end_date = bounds.end + timedelta(days=3)

    # Now you can use start_date and end_date to filter your data accordingly
    # For example, if you're querying a database, you would use these dates in your query
    # Here's a placeholder for the actual data fetching logic
    data = fetch_data(start_date, end_date)

    return data

def fetch_data(start_date, end_date) -> pd.DataFrame:
    # Placeholder function to represent fetching data between start_date and end_date
    # Replace this with your actual data fetching logic
    pass
```

To ensure that this asset runs on the fourth of the month, you would set up a schedule or sensor that triggers the materialization of the asset on that specific day. Here's an example of how you might define a schedule:

```python
from dagster import schedule

@schedule(cron="0 0 4 * *", job=my_monthly_asset_job, execution_timezone="UTC")
def my_monthly_asset_schedule(_context):
    # This function will be called on the fourth of every month at midnight UTC
    pass
```

Replace `my_monthly_asset_job` with the actual job that materializes your asset. The schedule will ensure that the asset computation is triggered on the fourth of every month, which will then process data from the first of the month to three days after the end of the month.

questions.title: Agent-level concurrency limitation for docker-based agents in Cloud Hybrid
questions.question:
Agent-level concurrency limitation for docker-based agents in Cloud Hybrid

Hi all!

I wonder if there is a way to limit how many runs can be sent to one particular Docker agent connected to our Dagster Hybrid Cloud.

Our usecase is pretty simple: 95% of the time we have very low workload, so it can be managed with pretty small agent machine.
However we occasionally perform huge loads, which requires way more processing power.

So my initial idea was to bring additional agent dedicated for this workloads and take it down as soon as load finished.
However I'm not quite sure how to manage jobs between agents to prevent accidental OOM-ing/killing smaller "day-to-day" agent.
I assumed there should be some way to limit number of jobs per agent, or make agent a priority target for some of the jobs by tags, but I cannot find anything related in docs or source code.
So hoping for any help here :)

Ps. I understand, that using K8s-based agents is, probably, a proper way to handle such computation scaling, however dealing with increased infrastructure complexity is not worth it right now for us.
questions.reference_answer:
Hi @mudravrik,

there's no agent scoped run limit unfortunately. 

> So my initial idea was to bring additional agent dedicated for this workloads and take it down as soon as load finished.

Unless you're taking down your small agent while your big agent is up, you would have to use agent queues to control which agents gets your job, which basically map a code location to a queue and by configuration agent can decide which queue they consume. Now that implies you can separate your huge workload from your small ones.

Then, if you can do that you might be able to achieve this by [limiting concurrency](https://docs.dagster.io/guides/limiting-concurrency-in-data-pipelines) at an appropriate level depending on your need.

Or a simpler path with some manual steps each time. Adjust [concurrency globally](https://docs.dagster.io/guides/limiting-concurrency-in-data-pipelines#limiting-overall-runs). 

IE, spin down small agent, spin down big agent, bump overall concurrency to whatever is needed, execute bulk jobs, then revert/scale down.

Both workarounds, seems either tedious or just another way to build up the complexity of your platform, so I am not particularly pleased to offer these avenues.


> Ps. I understand, that using K8s-based agents is, probably, a proper way to handle such computation scaling, however dealing with increased infrastructure complexity is not worth it right now for us.

You could also consider the ECS agent and achieve the same scalability goal for less time investment. k8s is more powerful but ECS+Fargate can definitely give you some decent mileage for relatively low effort.
questions.curate_reason: Chunks [2], [4], and [6] directly support the primary workaround of using agent queues and queue routing to separate workloads across multiple agents, which is the core solution suggested in the accepted answer.

questions.title: How to properly trigger a job once other are done?
questions.question:
How to properly trigger a job once other are done?

I have the following jobs:

```python
from dagster import AssetSelection
from dagster import define_asset_job

from dagster_sifflet import assets

load_hubspot_job = define_asset_job(
    name="load_hubspot_job", selection=assets.hubspot_assets
)

load_exchange_rates_job = define_asset_job(
    name="load_exchange_rates_job", selection=assets.exchange_rates_assets
)

load_tenants_job = define_asset_job(
    name="load_tenants_job", selection=assets.tenants_assets
)

run_dbt_job = define_asset_job(
    name="run_dbt_job",
    selection=AssetSelection.all()
    - AssetSelection.assets(*assets.exchange_rates_assets)
    - AssetSelection.assets(*assets.tenants_assets)
    - AssetSelection.assets(*assets.hubspot_assets),
)
```


And I would like the run_dbt_job to be run once the first 3 other ones are done, could you help me with that?
questions.reference_answer:
To get `run_dbt_job` to run after the completion of multiple other jobs (`load_hubspot_job`, `load_exchange_rates_job`, and `load_tenants_job`), you can use a sensor to monitor for successful runs and trigger `run_dbt_job`.

Here's what such a sensor might look like:

```python
from typing import Any
from dagster import sensor, RunRequest, RunsFilter, DagsterRunStatus, SkipReason, SensorEvaluationContext
from datetime import datetime


@sensor(job=run_dbt_job)
def dbt_job_sensor(context: SensorEvaluationContext) -> Any:
    # Define the jobs to monitor for completion
    monitored_jobs = [
        "load_hubspot_job",
        "load_exchange_rates_job",
        "load_tenants_job",
    ]

    # Retrieve the cursor which represents the last time we checked job runs
    last_cursor = context.cursor or "0"
    last_timestamp = datetime.fromtimestamp(float(last_cursor))

    # Check for successful completion of all monitored jobs since the last cursor timestamp
    for job_name in monitored_jobs:
        run_records = context.instance.get_run_records(
            filters=RunsFilter(
                job_name=job_name,
                statuses=[DagsterRunStatus.SUCCESS],
                created_after=last_timestamp,
            ),
            order_by="update_timestamp",
            ascending=False,
        )
        # If any job has not completed successfully since the last cursor timestamp, return without yielding a RunRequest
        if not run_records:
            return SkipReason(
                f"Job {job_name} has not completed successfully since {last_timestamp}"
            )

    # If all monitored jobs have completed successfully since the last cursor timestamp, yield a RunRequest for run_dbt_job
    context.update_cursor(str(datetime.utcnow().timestamp()))
    return RunRequest()

```

This sensor checks if all the specified jobs have completed successfully. If they have, it returns a `RunRequest`, otherwise it provides a `SkipReason` as to which job it's waiting on.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete Dagster configuration question with a substantive, code-backed answer; chunks [0] and [1] directly discuss using run status sensors to coordinate multiple independent jobs, and chunk [4] exemplifies the RunRequest pattern used in the answer.

airbyte+ dbt + dagster: dbt upstream dependency issue

Hello everyone,

I'm relatively new to Dagster and currently following a tutorial found here: [Airbyte + dbt + Dagster Quickstart](https://github.com/airbytehq/quickstarts/tree/main/airbyte_dbt_dagster).

I've successfully set up everything as per the tutorial, and everything works fine when I manually trigger materializations from the Dagster UI. All my models work well, the connections to Airbyte are triggered followed by the triggering of dbt models.

However, I encounter an issue when I attempt to utilize the scheduling function. My goal is to automate the workflow where Airbyte flows are triggered first, followed by dbt model materializations.

Here's the code snippet I'm using for scheduling:


```python
from dagster_dbt import build_schedule_from_dbt_selection
from .assets import dbt_project_dbt_assets

schedules = [
     build_schedule_from_dbt_selection(
         [dbt_project_dbt_assets],
         job_name="materialize_dbt_models",
         cron_schedule="0 0 * * *",
         dbt_select="fqn:*",
     ),
]
```

With this setup, I observe that a schedule is correctly created in the UI and it runs as expected. However, it only triggers the dbt materialization and not the upstream Airbyte flow, despite the UI showing that assets are correctly connected with lineage.

From my understanding, Dagster should orchestrate each asset's materialization along with all its upstream dependencies.

Here is other part of my script : 

asset.py
```python
import os
from dagster import AssetExecutionContext
from dagster_dbt import DbtCliResource, dbt_assets
from dagster_airbyte import AirbyteResource, load_assets_from_airbyte_instance

from .constants import dbt_manifest_path

airbyte_instance = AirbyteResource(
    host="localhost",
    port="8000",
    # If using basic auth, include username and password:
    username="airbyte",
    password=os.getenv("AIRBYTE_PASSWORD")
)

airbyte_assets = load_assets_from_airbyte_instance(airbyte_instance)


@dbt_assets(manifest=dbt_manifest_path)
def dbt_project_dbt_assets(context: AssetExecutionContext, dbt: DbtCliResource):
    yield from dbt.cli(["build"], context=context).stream()
```

constants.py
```python
import os
from pathlib import Path

from dagster_dbt import DbtCliResource

dbt_project_dir = Path(__file__).joinpath("..", "..", ".." , "dbt_project").resolve()
dbt = DbtCliResource(project_dir=os.fspath(dbt_project_dir))

# If DAGSTER_DBT_PARSE_PROJECT_ON_LOAD is set, a manifest will be created at run time.
# Otherwise, we expect a manifest to be present in the project's target directory.
if os.getenv("DAGSTER_DBT_PARSE_PROJECT_ON_LOAD"):
    dbt_manifest_path = (
        dbt.cli(
            ["--quiet", "parse"],
            target_path=Path("target"),
        )
        .wait()
        .target_path.joinpath("manifest.json")
    )
else:
    dbt_manifest_path = dbt_project_dir.joinpath("target", "manifest.json")
```

definitions.py
```python
import os

from dagster import Definitions
from dagster_dbt import DbtCliResource

from .assets import dbt_project_dbt_assets, airbyte_assets
from .constants import dbt_project_dir
from .schedules import schedules

defs = Definitions(
    assets=[dbt_project_dbt_assets, airbyte_assets],
    schedules=schedules,
    resources={
        "dbt": DbtCliResource(project_dir=os.fspath(dbt_project_dir)),
    },
)
```

Am I required to schedule my Airbyte flows separately?
Or am I missing something in my current setup?
Any guidance or suggestions would be greatly appreciated. Thank you in advance for your help!
questions.reference_answer:
I suspect that you need to update the `schedule.py` to include the Airbyte flows specifically. 

In `assets.py` the code creates both the dbt assets via `dbt_project_dbt_assets` and the upstream Airbyte assets using the [load_assets_from_airbyte_instance function](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/airbyte#loading-airbyte-asset-definitions-from-an-airbyte-instance). However the schedule definition only invokes `dbt_project_dbt_assets`. 

[Our documentation shows example schedules for Airbyte syncs](https://docs.dagster.io/integrations/airbyte#step-4-scheduling-airbyte-syncs). It doesn't use dbt, but it does specify the use of `airbyte_assets` in the schedule definition. Update your `schedule.py` similarly and give that a try! 

```python
from dagster import define_asset_job, ScheduleDefinition, Definitions

# Materialize all assets
run_everything_job = define_asset_job("run_everything", selection="*")

# Define the schedule for the job
schedule = ScheduleDefinition(
    job=run_everything_job,
    cron_schedule="0 0 * * *",  # Daily at midnight
)

# Combine assets and schedules into a Definitions object
defs = Definitions(
    assets=[airbyte_assets, dbt_project_dbt_assets],
    schedules=[schedule],
)
```
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [2] directly addresses scheduling jobs containing dbt assets with non-dbt assets (Airbyte in this case), which is the exact problem and solution pattern presented in the accepted answer.

questions.title: Create dynamic assets on runtime
questions.question:
Create dynamic assets on runtime

It feels like Dagster has been pushing for software-defined assed directions (which is a right direction in my opinion) but I am still struggling to wrap my head about how to do some stuff being used to a more op/job mindset.

Right now it's kind of easy to imlpement the following logic using ops/jobs/graphs:

1. List all of the credentials in a path.
2. For each credential, create an op (and the downstream) to do some computations that reads data from a database.
3. At the end materialize the data in a file.

The pseudo code is something along the lines (please understand that is **pseudo** code)

```python
import os
from dagster import op, job, DynamicOut, DynamicOutput


@op(out=DynamicOut())
def get_client_credentials(context):
    """
    Creates downstream op for every client whose credentials are on the creds/ path.
    """
    for creds_file in os.listdir("/creds/"):
        with open(creds_file, 'r') as fr:
            yield DynamicOut(value=fr.read(),mapping_key=creds_file)

@op
def get_invoices(creds:str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Extracts credentials to connect to a database and returns a dataframe as the result from the invoice's query.
    """
    user,pwd = creds.split(",")
    connection = connect(user,pwd)
    return connection.query(f"SELECT * FROM invoices WHERE client_name={creds_file}")

@op
def get_max_invoice(context,invoices:pd.DataFrame) -> None:
    """
    Stores in S3 on a specific path per client the max invoice in the invoices dataframe.
    """
    bucket = boto.s3()
    invoices_max = invoices.max()
    bucket.write(path=f"{context.get_mapping_key()}/max",data=invoices_max)


@job
def put_max_to_s3():
     clients_creds = get_client_credentials()
     client_creds.map(get_invoices).map(get_max_invoice)
```

This has several benefits, so I can add as many clients as I want to the folder `creds/` without having to modify any of my code, the downstream jobs run in parallel somewhat, I can easil identify which ops for each client are failing and some of the dynamic created ops will run until the end even if a specific client fails.

Now I am not sure how to implement this althought I have been looking at some discussions such as [#9559](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/9559) but I have really struggled to understand the examples and definitions given.

And also saw some comments such as

> - software-defined assets currently don't support dynamic asset keys, and it's unlikely we will in the future. However, we will likely add support for dynamic partition keys, which is the solution proposed in the issue description above.

What's the right mindset to approach this based on the software asset definitions?

**EDIT**

Just came across [#11045](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/11045), but still would be interested on having a more similiar example to the one I posted, given that if you really need to generate multiple linked assets it seems a bit complex as show in 11045, and still have some flaws: not sure how to access the context inside the asset (given that it should be passed to the factory?) What happens if I want to create dynamically two assets that are dependent between them? I cant pass them as argument to the function (Python does not allow the creation of dynamic arguments in its functions).

Similarly [#15087](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/15807) shows what seem to be a too complex way to do it given how easy is to do so with ops.
questions.reference_answer:
In general, if you have large numbers of objects with the same structure, we recommend using partitions instead of using a separate asset for each object.  This makes it easier to perform bulk operations like backfills, as well as makes the UI more scalable to navigate. 
 Here's some content on this: https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/12061.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete question about implementing a specific pattern with assets, substantive answer recommending partitions as the approach, and chunk [2] directly discusses partitions including dynamic partitioning which is relevant to the recommended solution.

questions.title: Confused about code locations and kubernetes
questions.question:
Confused about code locations and kubernetes

I have been experimenting with dagster locally and everything works great. I now want to move my pipelines to kubernetes. I have installed the main helm chart, and I have separately deployed a code location with a grpc endpoint. The code location contains a trivial single asset that returns 'Hello'. I have connected dagster  to the code location, using the values.yml (workspace/servers), and in the UI I can see the asset. However when I try to manually materialize it, I am getting the below error.

I was naively thinking that the asset would be materialized in the code location pod, via an instruction sent over the grpc interface. But it looks like it is trying to spin up a new pod, as it is using the k8s client and seems to be compaining that it doesnt know which image to use.

I have read through the docs, but it is quite confusing...

I have two basic questions, which I think would get me along the path to understanding how this all works : 

1. How do I configure my dagster deployment and code location deployment so that the asset is materialized in the code location deployment, without spinning up new pods.
2. How do I configure my dagster deployment and code location deployment so that the asset is materialized in a new k8s pod (i.e. fixing the error I am currently seeing).

questions.reference_answer:
Yes, that's right.

Also, for your first question, you can configure your run launcher to be the [DefaultRunLauncher](https://docs.dagster.io/_apidocs/internals#dagster._core.launcher.DefaultRunLauncher) instead of the `K8sRunLauncher`.  The default run launcher launches the run as a separate process on the GRPC server, rather than launching a k8s pod for the run.  You'd have to configure it in helm as a `customRunLauncher`.
